OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old male patient, with a known history of
 
 human immunodeficiency virus
 
 and
 
 hepatitis C virus
 
 coinfection, underwent a computed tomography (CT) to assess liver lesions detected by US, which were later identified as haemangiomas. On physical examination, hypoplastic fingernails were found.
 Imaging Findings: Incidentally, the CT revealed the presence of bilateral osseous processes projecting from the posterior aspect of the iliac bones (Figures 1a and 1b). Anteroposterior and lateral knee radiographs showed bilateral patellar hypoplasia associated with luxation (Figure 2a), while elbow radiographs demonstrated bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature (Figure 2b).
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old male patient, with a known history of human immunodeficiency virus and hepatitis C virus coinfection, underwent a computed tomography (CT) to assess liver lesions detected by US, which were later identified as haemangiomas. On physical examination, hypoplastic fingernails were found.
 Imaging Findings: Incidentally, the CT revealed the presence of bilateral osseous processes projecting from the posterior aspect of the iliac bones. Anteroposterior and lateral knee radiographs showed bilateral patellar hypoplasia associated with luxation, while elbow radiographs demonstrated bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radio-humeral dislocation and abnormal radial curvature.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Nail–patella syndrome, Coffin–Siris syndrome, Meier–Gorlin syndrome, Coxopodopatellar syndrome, Genitopatellar syndrome
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 46-year-old male with HIV and hepatitis C coinfection (likely incidental to the case, not directly pathogenic).
- Hypoplastic fingernails on physical examination.
- Bilateral osteolytic or bone processes at the posterior iliac bones on CT.
- Bilateral patellar hypoplasia with luxation on knee radiographs.
- Bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radioulnar dislocation and abnormal radial curvature on elbow radiographs.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:
1) Nail–patella syndrome:
   Pros: Classic features include hypoplastic or absent nails, patellar hypoplasia or aplasia often bilateral, radial head dislocations or hypoplasia, and iliac horns (bilateral bony projections from posterior iliac bones) on pelvis imaging. The findings here match all classic radiographic hallmarks.
   Cons: None significant; perfect clinical and imaging correlation.

2) Coffin–Siris syndrome:
   Pros: May have nail abnormalities.
   Cons: Typically includes coarse facial features, hirsutism, developmental delay, and absent or hypoplastic distal phalanges of the fifth fingers/toes, but does not classically involve patellar or radial head hypoplasia or iliac horns. The radiographic features described are not characteristic.

3) Meier–Gorlin syndrome:
   Pros: Characterized by microtia (small ears), patellar hypoplasia, and dwarfism.
   Cons: No mention of nail hypoplasia or iliac horns; radial head abnormalities not typical. The presence of hypoplastic nails and iliac horns favor Nail-patella over Meier-Gorlin.

4) Coxopodopatellar syndrome:
   Pros: Includes patellar hypoplasia and limb anomalies.
   Cons: Very rare, usually includes other severe anomalies such as genital malformations, ambiguous genitalia, and spinal anomalies; no typical involvement of nail hypoplasia or radial head dislocation; iliac horns not described in this syndrome.

5) Genitopatellar syndrome:
   Pros: Includes patellar hypoplasia or absent patellae, along with genitourinary anomalies, and sometimes radioulnar anomalies.
   Cons: Typically includes severe developmental delay, renal and urinary tract anomalies; no description of nail hypoplasia or iliac horns; radial head hypoplasia less typical. The presence of iliac horns and nail hypoplasia again points away.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:
- Nail–patella syndrome fits perfectly with the classic tetrad of nail hypoplasia, bilateral patellar hypoplasia with luxation, bilateral radial head hypoplasia with radioulnar dislocation, and characteristic iliac horns. This is pathognomonic.
- Among the others, none have the combination of all findings, and the presence of iliac horns is specific to Nail–patella syndrome.

</analysis>

<final> Nail–patella syndrome </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male with a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presents to the emergency department with a one-week onset of orthopnoea and exertional dyspnoea. Laboratory tests demonstrate elevated troponins, the electrocardiogram reveals a third-degree atrioventricular block, and the echocardiogram shows right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.
 Imaging Findings: A PET-CT scan (Figure 1) was carried out revealing a large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue. It extended through the right chambers and around the large vessels, sparing the left ventricle. Additionally, a hypermetabolic nodular image was identified at the left lung base.
 
 Therefore, a contrast-enhanced chest CT scan was performed (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c and 2d). It confirmed the presence of an infiltrative hypodense mass in the myocardium. It involved the right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove. The mass caused mural thickening of the right cavities. A cuff encircled the right coronary artery along its course. A moderate pericardial effusion was also observed. Finally, the CT scan confirmed the solid nodule in the posterior segment of the left lower lobe.
 
 A CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of the lung nodule was made for histological examination.
PostDescription:

It confirmed the presence of an infiltrative hypodense mass in the myocardium. It involved the right atrium, right ventricle, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove. The mass caused mural thickening of the right cavities. A cuff encircled the right coronary artery along its course. A moderate pericardial effusion was also observed. Finally, the CT scan confirmed the solid nodule in the posterior segment of the left lower lobe. A CT-guided percutaneous biopsy of the lung nodule was made for histological examination.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Angiosarcoma, Ig-G4 related disease, Primary cardiac lymphoma, Erdheim–Chester disease, Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
1) An 81-year-old male with new-onset orthopnoea, exertional dyspnoea, elevated troponins, and third-degree AV block.  
2) Echocardiography shows right ventricular thickening and pericardial effusion.  
3) PET-CT: Large hypermetabolic myocardial tissue mass involving right cardiac chambers (right atrium, right ventricle), interatrial septum, and atrioventricular groove; sparing left ventricle; encircling right coronary artery.  
4) Moderate pericardial effusion.  
5) Hypermetabolic lung nodule in left lower lobe confirmed on CT, biopsied for histology.  
B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Angiosarcoma:  
   - Pros: Angiosarcoma is the most common primary malignant cardiac tumor, frequently involving the right atrium. Hypermetabolic PET-CT findings with infiltrative myocardial mass and pericardial effusion are consistent.   
   - Cons: No mention of hemorrhagic or necrotic areas typical of angiosarcomas; however, this is not exclusive.  
2) Ig-G4 related disease:  
   - Pros: Can involve pericardium and myocardium with infiltrative lesions; generally more indolent, may have pericardial effusion.  
   - Cons: Usually shows diffuse thickening rather than focal mass sparing left ventricle; hypermetabolic PET uptake is less typical; no systemic signs or other organ involvement; not commonly associated with AV block or lung nodules.  
3) Primary cardiac lymphoma:  
   - Pros: Typically presents with right-sided cardiac infiltration, can cause conduction abnormalities, pericardial effusion, and mass lesion; hypermetabolic on PET; lung involvement possible as secondary site; older patients predisposed (often patients with immunodeficiency, but can occur de novo).  
   - Cons: Less commonly causes nodular lung lesions as a primary manifestation but possible if metastasis or synchronous involvement.  
4) Erdheim–Chester disease:  
   - Pros: Histioiocytic disorder with retroperitoneal, pericardial involvement; can cause pericardial effusion and myocardial infiltration.  
   - Cons: Usually involves the left ventricle and interventricular septum diffusely; cardiac involvement is typically symmetric and diffuse rather than a focal infiltrative mass; lung nodules less common.  
5) Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma:  
   - Pros: Can present as large infiltrative cardiac mass; can involve right chambers.  
   - Cons: Typically primary heart tumors, less likely to metastasize synchronously to lungs at presentation; histology would confirm; PET uptake possible but less commonly hypermetabolic nodules in lung base at presentation.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Primary cardiac lymphoma  
   - Reason: The combination of right-sided massive myocardial infiltrative hypermetabolic mass, conduction disturbance (third-degree AV block), pericardial effusion, and synchronous hypermetabolic lung nodule (biopsy-proven site) fits well with lymphoma which can involve heart and lungs. The lesion sparing left ventricle and encircling right coronary artery also matches typical lymphoma spread.  
2) Angiosarcoma  
   - Reason: Most common primary malignant cardiac tumor, often in right atrium with pericardial effusion and infiltration; but lung nodules are less characteristic unless metastasis, and no necrosis or hemorrhage mentioned.  
3) Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma  
   - Reason: Possible but less likely with synchronous lung nodule and the described pattern.  
4) Erdheim–Chester disease  
   - Reason: Less likely due to asymmetric involvement sparing left ventricle and nodular lung lesion.  
5) Ig-G4 related disease  
   - Reason: Clinical and imaging features less supportive.  
  
Based on imaging characteristics (right-sided infiltrative hypermetabolic mass, pericardial effusion, conduction block, and lung nodule) and clinical context, primary cardiac lymphoma is the most consistent diagnosis.  
</analysis>  
<final>Primary cardiac lymphoma</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 37-year-old man presented to our department of surgery with complains of high-grade fever, chills, vomiting and abdominal pain in epigastric region for the last 7 days. Laboratory investigations revealed a raised total leukocyte count (TLC) (of 18,500 cells/μL) with increased absolute neutrophil count (80%), and mildly deranged alkaline phosphate levels (200 IU/L).
 Imaging Findings: Initial abdominal ultrasonography (USG) revealed hepatomegaly with a large left lobe liver abscess (350 cc) and signs of impending rupture. Mild prominence of intrahepatic bile ducts (Figures 1a and 1b) and a few intrahepatic veno-venous collaterals (measuring ~2 mm in diameter) were also noted between the branch of the middle and right hepatic vein (Figures 2a, 2b and 6).
 
 Doppler study showed echogenic content with absent colour flow at the porto-splenic confluence, suggesting thrombosis, along with collateral vessel formation at the porta hepatis, likely indicative of cavernous transformation (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, 4a and 4b).
 
 Contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) scan of the abdomen confirmed the abscess and thrombosis, additionally revealing pinpoint narrowing of the inferior vena cava (IVC) with a small segment thrombus (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d and 5e). Notably, pancreatic parenchymal varices were observed (involving the head, and body of the pancreas) draining directly into the splenic vein (Figures 3e and 4c).
 
 Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) was also performed (Figures 7 and 8), revealing prominent intrahepatic bile ducts in the left lobe of the liver and non-visualisation of the biliary channels distal to the common hepatic duct (CHD) consistent with biliary obstruction.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 37-year-old man with high-grade fever, chills, vomiting, and epigastric pain for 7 days.  
- Raised TLC (18,500 cells/Î¼L) with neutrophilia (80%) and mildly raised alkaline phosphatase (200 IU/L).  
- USG shows hepatomegaly, large left lobe liver abscess (350 cc), signs of impending rupture.  
- Doppler reveals echogenic content, absent flow at porto-splenic confluence indicating thrombosis, and collateral vessels at porta hepatis suggesting cavernous transformation.  
- CECT confirms abscess, thrombosis, and pinpoint narrowing of IVC with small segment thrombus.  
- Pancreatic parenchymal varices noted in head and body of pancreas draining into splenic vein.  
- MRCP shows prominent intrahepatic bile ducts in left lobe and non-visualization distal to CHD, suggesting biliary obstruction.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices formation:  
   - Pros: Varices, collateral formation, and thrombosis can occur in portal hypertension; biliary obstruction and hepatomegaly possible.  
   - Cons: No mention of cirrhosis findings (no nodular liver contour, fibrosis signs), patient is young with acute presentation; liver abscess not typical in cirrhosis; cavernous transformation and IVC narrowing not classic for cirrhosis.  
2) Arteriovenous malformation (AVM):  
   - Pros: AVM can cause vascular changes and thrombosis.  
   - Cons: No imaging findings suggestive of AVM, no abnormal vascular tangle or early venous filling, no prior AVM symptoms; liver abscess and thrombosis not typical for AVM.  
3) Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess:  
   - Pros: Pylephlebitis is septic thrombophlebitis of portal vein typically secondary to intra-abdominal infection; liver abscess can lead to pylephlebitis; presence of pancreatic parenchymal varices explained by collateral formation due to portal vein thrombosis. Imaging findings of abscess, porto-splenic confluence thrombosis, cavernous transformation, and pancreatic varices fit.  
   - Cons: None significant; fits all findings.  
4) Cystic lesions of pancreas:  
   - Pros: Pancreatic cystic lesions can cause mass effect.  
   - Cons: No cystic lesions described; main lesion is liver abscess, pancreatic involvement is varices, not cystic lesions.  
5) Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms (IPMN):  
   - Pros: Can cause pancreatic duct dilation, cystic changes.  
   - Cons: No imaging signs of IPMN (no ductal mucus, cystic neoplasm); pancreatic findings are varices, not neoplasm.  
  
C) Ranking:  
1) Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess – fits all findings well, classic for septic portal vein thrombosis with collateral formation and pancreatic varices.  
2) Liver cirrhosis with portal hypertension and varices formation – less likely due to lack of cirrhosis findings and acute infection.  
3) Arteriovenous malformation – unlikely given lack of AVM imaging features.  
4) Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms – no evidence of neoplasm.  
5) Cystic lesions of pancreas – no cystic pancreatic lesions described.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Pylephlebitis and pancreatic parenchymal varices secondary to hepatic abscess  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 31-year-old male presented at the urologist with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks. He had no relevant history. Clinically there was a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis. Blood results showed extremely high alpha-fetoprotein levels >53.000 µg/L.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation (Figures 1, 2 and 3). There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown).
 
 CT showed, besides the large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum (Figure 4), no metastasis or lymphadenopathies.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 31-year-old male presented at the urologist with scrotal swelling for 4 weeks. He had no relevant history. Clinically there was a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis. Blood results showed extremely high alpha-fetoprotein levels >53.000 µg/L.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation. There was no visualisation of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right. There was a normal left testis and epididymis (not shown). CT showed, besides the large heterogeneous mass in the right hemiscrotum, no metastasis or lymphadenopathies.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Mixed germinal cell tumour, Pure yolk sac tumour, Seminoma, Choriocarcinoma, Teratoma, Lymphoma

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 31-year-old male with a voluminous solid swelling of the right hemiscrotum and retraction of the penis.  
- Ultrasound shows a large heterogeneous mass with solid and cystic/necrotic components and vascularisation in the right hemiscrotum, with no visualization of normal testicular parenchyma or epididymis on the right.  
- Normal left testis and epididymis.  
- Blood results: extremely high alpha-fetoprotein (>53,000 µg/L).  
- CT shows no metastasis or lymphadenopathy.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Mixed germinal cell tumour  
   - Pros: Common testicular tumour in young males; can present as heterogeneous mass with solid and cystic components.  
   - Cons: AFP is often elevated, but the AFP value is extremely high here, and no metastasis is seen (which can be present in mixed GCT). Mixed GCT includes several subtypes (seminoma, yolk sac, embryonal carcinoma, choriocarcinoma), but very high AFP suggests a major yolk sac component.

2) Pure yolk sac tumour  
   - Pros: Typically associated with very high AFP levels (often >10,000 µg/L), matches the extremely high AFP (>53,000 µg/L). Usually presents as a solid, heterogeneous mass in the testis. Common in younger patients (<30 years).  
   - Cons: No metastasis seen (though metastases can be absent). Imaging fits.  

3) Seminoma  
   - Pros: Common testicular tumour in this age group, usually presents as a homogeneous mass.  
   - Cons: AFP is typically normal or slightly elevated; very high AFP is not typical. The mass is heterogeneous with cystic/necrotic areas, less typical.

4) Choriocarcinoma  
   - Pros: Can elevate tumor markers (hCG high, sometimes AFP).  
   - Cons: AFP is rarely elevated; hCG is the main marker. The description does not mention hCG or typical hemorrhagic areas. Very high AFP is not typical.

5) Teratoma  
   - Pros: Can appear heterogeneous with cystic/solid components.  
   - Cons: AFP is not typically extremely high. Usually seen in older patients or in mixed GCT context.

6) Lymphoma  
   - Pros: Testicular lymphoma can present as a large mass.  
   - Cons: AFP is not elevated in lymphoma. Very high AFP excludes lymphoma.

C) Ranking:  
1) Pure yolk sac tumour - fits the extremely high AFP (>53,000 µg/L), young age, and heterogeneous mass with solid and cystic components.  
2) Mixed germinal cell tumour - plausible but less specific than pure yolk sac tumour given the AFP levels.  
3) Seminoma - less likely due to AFP elevation and heterogeneous features.  
4) Teratoma - less likely due to AFP.  
5) Choriocarcinoma - less likely due to AFP.  
6) Lymphoma - excluded due to AFP elevation.

The clinical and imaging findings strongly support a pure yolk sac tumour as the diagnosis.  
</analysis>  
<final>Pure yolk sac tumour</final><|return|>

 anterior communicating (
 
 ACom) artery. The foetal origin of the left
 
 posterior cerebral artery (

 The patient is
 
 HBsAg
 
 positive; otherwise, all laboratory findings were within the normal limits. Neurological and other systemic examinations were within the normal limits and no cranial nerve palsy was detected.
 Imaging Findings: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) brain with contrast was done to find the aetiology behind it, and it revealed a lesion in the supratentorial compartment, centred within the deep white matter in the right frontal cortex.
 
 A cerebrospinal
 
 fluid (C
 
 SF) culture was done, and it showed an oligoclonal band suggestive of intrathecal IgG antibody synthesis. Patient was started on salmeterol and clinicians finds significant improvement in seizures.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 51-year-old female patient, a farmer, presented with two years history of abdominal pain, vomits, diarrhoea, asthenia, weight loss (12%), and arthralgias. Laboratory findings showed
 
 iron deficiency anaemia
 
 , increased erythrocyte sedimentation rate, mild leukocytosis, thrombocytosis, C-reactive protein 6 mg/dL
 
 (normal value < 0.5 mg/dL)
 
 and hypoalbuminemia 2.5 g/dL.
 Imaging Findings: Abdominal-pelvic computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed cystadenocarcinoma of the right ovary with
 
 suspicion of lymph node metastases
 
 and the patient was referred to our oncology institute.
 
 The exams were re-assessed and the malignant ovarian tumour was excluded, as the MRI was highly suggestive of cystadenofibroma
 
 (Figure 1).
 
 In addition, the CT demonstrated low-attenuation lymph nodes (fatty infiltration) in the left supraclavicular region, mediastinum, retroperitoneum and mesentery
 
 (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 2d, 3a, and 3b). Furthermore, prominent jejunal folds were seen on T2-weighted images (Figure 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old man with lower abdominal pain received a CT abdomen/pelvis in the emergency department to rule out appendicitis. An incidental fat attenuation mass was present in the right ischiorectal fossa. Five years later, the patient presented again with pelvic pain and received a CT pelvis.
 Imaging Findings: The initial CT study identified a right ischiorectal fossa fat attenuation mass displacing the anal canal towards the left without enhancing soft tissue, fluid or calcifications (Figures 1a and 1b). There was a thin capsule surrounding the mass.
 
 When the patient presented again five years later, a CT pelvis showed that the mass had significantly enlarged (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). It contained two well-defined cystic compartments with homogeneous fluid attenuation (15 HU) and an irregular enhancing soft tissue rim in the posterosuperior compartment.
 
 An MR pelvis done shortly thereafter recapitulated these findings but revealed that the apparently homogeneous central collection on CT contained a mix of fluid and soft tissue, with both thin and thick septations (Figures 3a, 3b, 4a, and 4b). Additionally, the entire mass had a thick, irregular rim of enhancement.
PostDescription:

An incidental fat attenuation mass was present in the right ischiorectal fossa. Five years later, the patient presented again with pelvic pain and received a CT pelvis.
 Imaging Findings: The initial CT study identified a right ischiorectal fossa fat attenuation mass displacing the anal canal towards the left without enhancing soft tissue, fluid or calcifications. There was a thin capsule surrounding the mass. When the patient presented again five years later, a CT pelvis showed that the mass had significantly enlarged. It contained two well-defined cystic compartments with homogeneous fluid attenuation (15 HU) and an irregular enhancing soft tissue rim in the posterosuperior compartment. An MR pelvis done shortly thereafter recapitulated these findings but revealed that the apparently homogeneous central collection on CT contained a mix of fluid and soft tissue, with both thin and thick septations. Additionally, the entire mass had a thick, irregular rim of enhancement.

- Mass displaced anal canal to left, no enhancement, fluid, or calcifications initially; thin capsule noted.
- Five years later, mass significantly enlarged, now with two cystic compartments (15 HU fluid), irregular enhancing soft tissue rim in posterosuperior compartment.
- MRI shows heterogeneous central collection with fluid and soft tissue, septations (thin and thick), and thick irregular rim of enhancement.
- Location: right ischiorectal fossa (perineal area), no mention of extension through sacrum or spine.
- No evidence of solid enhancing nodules initially, but later enhancement and complex contents.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old female patient was admitted to our oncology centre with follicular lymphoma. She had a seven-kilogram weight loss over one year, loss of appetite, and fever of unknown origin. There is no significant medical history, and physical examination revealed only cervical lymphadenopathy.
 
 The patient’s blood workup revealed a slight increase in C-reactive protein (1.2 mg/dL) and creatine kinase (151 U/L), with no other abnormal laboratory findings.
 Imaging Findings: Computed tomography detected a large lesion, measuring 7.5 x 5 centimetres, heterogeneous, with fluid densities and lobulated margins in the superior segments of the right hepatic lobe (Figure 1). Due to the
 
 patient’s
 
 clinical context, an ultrasound-guided biopsy was performed, which revealed an unrepresentative fragment of liver tissue, encompassing a fibro-hyalinised nodule.
 
 An abdominal magnetic resonance (MR) was performed to characterise the hepatic lesion further (Figures 2, 3a, 3b, and 4). The MR revealed a heterogeneous, complex cystic lesion with a thin wall, hypointense on T2-weighted images, and hyperintense on T1-weighted images, located within the right liver lobe. The lesion exhibited radiating septa towards the periphery, appearing as low-signal intensity on T2-weighted images and high-signal intensity on T1-weighted images. These septa delineated multiple smaller cysts, hypointense on T1 and hyperintense on T2-weighted images, occupying almost the entire lesion. After contrast administration, the lesion did not exhibit restricted diffusion and no significant enhancement of the soft tissue component.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A one-year-old full-term-born male child, born to non-consanguineous parents, presents with complaints of thoracic deformity. No history of previous surgeries.
 Imaging Findings: The AP chest radiograph (Figure 1) demonstrated the absence of ribs in the right mid-zone, with fusion of multiple adjacent ribs. Fusion of multiple ribs was also noted on the left side. The image showed evidence of dextroscoliosis.
 
 Further evaluation with computed tomography revealed bifid ribs with segmental fusion and widening at multiple levels bilaterally (Videos and Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, 3, 4, and 5).
 
 Fusion of posterior vertebral elements was observed at multiple levels, and dextroscoliosis was noted involving the thoracic spine. Posterior vertebral arch defects were identified involving the L5 vertebra and all sacral vertebrae (Figures 2b and 2c).
 
 Mild volume loss of the right hemithorax was noted. The pulmonary parenchyma, bronchial, and vascular markings appeared normal bilaterally.

Clinical History: An otherwise healthy 22-year-old man presents with acute respiratory symptoms and has an emergency CT scan performed, which reveals an incidental finding.
 Imaging Findings: No abnormalities are evident on a PA radiograph of the chest (Figure 1). The CT reveals no thoracic abnormalities except for two bilateral cortical defects of the undersurface of the sternal extremity of each clavicle, being slightly larger on the left side (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c). An MRI is additionally performed, revealing no evidence of a pathological fracture, soft tissue mass or bone marrow oedema (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c).

- MRI shows no pathological fracture, soft tissue mass, or bone marrow edema.

1) Osteolytic metastasis  
- Pros: Cortical defects could represent lytic lesions.  
- Cons: Unlikely in a healthy 22-year-old man with no known primary malignancy; also bilateral symmetric cortical defects without soft tissue mass or marrow edema argue against metastases, which are usually asymmetric and often cause marrow edema or mass effect.  
2) Osteomyelitis  
- Pros: Bone destruction could cause cortical defects.  
- Cons: No soft tissue swelling, no marrow edema on MRI, and no clinical signs of infection; also bilateral symmetric presentation unlikely for osteomyelitis.  
3) Plasmacytoma  
- Pros: Can cause lytic lesions in bone.  
- Cons: Very unlikely in a 22-year-old; also no marrow edema or soft tissue mass; bilateral symmetric lesions uncommon.  
4) Giant cell tumour  
- Pros: Can cause lytic cortical lesions.  
- Cons: Usually epiphyseal/metaphyseal and often occurs in adults 20-40 years old, but bilateral symmetric lesions in the clavicle are highly unusual, and no soft tissue mass or edema observed.  
5) Rhomboid fossa variant  
- Pros: Rhomboid fossa is a known benign anatomic variant where there is a concave defect of the sternal surface of the clavicle; typically bilateral and symmetrical; no associated soft tissue mass or marrow edema.  
- Cons: None significant; this diagnosis fits the imaging and clinical picture well.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 17-month-old boy presented with a 3-day history of sternal swelling and pain. No fever or any
 
 other
 
 symptoms. There was no history of trauma. Physical examination revealed a 2 cm swelling in the manubriosternal region. Inflammatory parameters were normal except for
 
 a slightly elevated value for C-reactive protein.
 Imaging Findings: The chest radiograph revealed a presternal soft tissue swelling without calcifications and with no evidence of bony anomalies at the sternum, such as bone erosions or a periosteal reaction (Figure 1).
 
 A CT scan showed a fusiform soft tissue density lesion, centred on the manubriosternal joint, involving both superficial and deep planes of the synchondrosis, with subcutaneous and mediastinal extension, measuring 46 x 27 x 37 mm. No significant irregularity of the bony surfaces at the manubriosternal synchondrosis is visible.
 
 However, a separation between the manubrial and sternal ossification centres is evident
 
 , accompanied by densification of the adjacent subcutaneous tissue (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Ultrasound revealed a hypoechoic and heterogeneous, dumbbell-shaped lesion centred on the manubriosternal synchondrosis. This lesion crosses the synchondrosis, extending superficially into the subcutaneous tissue and deeply to the depth of the chest wall/anterior mediastinum. Colour Doppler ultrasound demonstrated no internal vascularisation. Additionally, there is a thickening of the soft tissues surrounding the manubriosternal joint (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c].
 
 Bone scintigraphy showed no significant abnormalities.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A newborn with ambiguous genitalia and multiple congenital anomalies was admitted in March 2023. Born preterm at 34 weeks, the infant faced respiratory distress, electrolyte imbalances, and sepsis.
 Imaging Findings: The patient presented with extensive congenital anomalies, including an omphalocele with liver and bowel herniating through a membrane-covered defect, bladder exstrophy, and imperforate anus.
 
 Spinal imaging revealed fusion defects in the lower thoracic and lumbar vertebrae, with an associated cystic mass with dystrophic neural elements. Cranial imaging showed a low-lying torcular Herophili, ventriculomegaly, fullness of the foramen magnum, and possible aqueductal stenosis. Other imaging findings included ectopic kidneys (one partially thoracic and another pelvic), midgut malrotation, sacral agenesis, rib synostosis, pubic symphysis diastasis, ambiguous genitalia, and other musculoskeletal abnormalities, highlighting extensive multi-system involvement.
 
 Due to the patient’s medical instability and eventual demise, as well as lack of resources, further imaging could not assess other abnormalities including undescended testes and detailed evaluation of the urinary system. These structural abnormalities posed considerable diagnostic and management challenges.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 85-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presented with a rapidly growing mass on her left shoulder (Figure 1), with no history of trauma.
 
 On physical examination, the mass was hard and non-mobile.
 Imaging Findings: A shoulder X-ray and ultrasound scan were performed in order to rule out malignancy.
 
 The X-ray (Figure 2) shows a rounded, homogeneous soft-tissue density mass on the left shoulder, located just above the acromioclavicular joint (ACJ). Additionally, the humeral head is seen cranially displaced, with subsequent narrowing of the subacromial space. Osseous degenerative change can be seen in both the humeral head and the ACJ.
 
 On the US exam (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c), the images show a supraclavicular fluid collection, predominantly anechogenic but heterogeneous, that appears to be erupting superiorly from the acromioclavicular (AC) interval. No Doppler signal is detected. On interrogation of the rotator cuff, the exam shows the absence of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons over the humeral head, as well as bony contour irregularity.
PostDescription:

The X-ray shows a rounded, homogeneous soft-tissue density mass on the left shoulder, located just above the acromioclavicular joint (ACJ). Additionally, the humeral head is seen cranially displaced, with subsequent narrowing of the subacromial space. Osseous degenerative change can be seen in both the humeral head and the ACJ. On the US exam, the images show a supraclavicular fluid collection, predominantly anechogenic but heterogeneous, that appears to be erupting superiorly from the acromioclavicular (AC) interval. No Doppler signal is detected. On interrogation of the rotator cuff, the exam shows the absence of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons over the humeral head, as well as bony contour irregularity.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Acromioclavicular joint traumatic injury, Degenerative acromioclavicular joint changes, Acromioclavicular cyst, Soft-tissue tumour, Acromioclavicular cyst secondary to massive rotator cuff tear
FinalDiagnosis: Acromioclavicular cyst secondary to massive rotator cuff tear
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Elderly woman (85 years old) with a rapidly growing, hard, non-mobile mass on the left shoulder.  
- X-ray: Rounded, homogeneous soft-tissue density mass above the ACJ, humeral head cranially displaced, narrowing subacromial space, osseous degenerative changes in humeral head and ACJ.  
- Ultrasound: Supraclavicular fluid collection, predominantly anechoic but heterogeneous, erupting superiorly from the AC interval, no Doppler signal. Rotator cuff interrogation shows absence of supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons over humeral head and bony contour irregularity.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Acromioclavicular joint traumatic injury  
   - Pros: Mass near ACJ, displacement of humeral head could be trauma-related.  
   - Cons: No history of trauma, rapidly growing mass with cystic fluid collection, rotator cuff tendons absent (trauma alone unlikely to cause massive rotator cuff tear at this age without prior history).  

2) Degenerative acromioclavicular joint changes  
   - Pros: Degenerative changes present.  
   - Cons: Degeneration alone does not explain rapidly growing, cystic mass or massive rotator cuff tear with soft tissue fluid collection; mass is cystic and not purely bony degenerative changes.  

3) Acromioclavicular cyst.  
   - Pros: Soft tissue mass over ACJ, anechoic/homogeneous fluid collection from AC interval.  
   - Cons: Does not explain absent rotator cuff tendons or massive cuff tear; cysts can be primary but less likely with massive rotator cuff tear and joint degeneration.  

4) Soft-tissue tumour  
   - Pros: Hard, non-mobile mass, rapid growth.  
   - Cons: Imaging shows cystic fluid collection, no Doppler signal (less likely vascular tumor), no evidence of solid heterogeneity; mass appears anechoic/soft tissue density rather than solid tumor; rotator cuff tear-related findings more consistent.  

5) Acromioclavicular cyst secondary to massive rotator cuff tear  
   - Pros: Mass above ACJ, cystic fluid collection, massive rotator cuff tendon loss (supra- and infraspinatus absent), humeral head cranial migration and subacromial narrowing consistent with cuff tear arthropathy; cyst fluid erupts from AC interval, degenerated joint and humeral head changes.  
   - Cons: None prominently conflicting.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Acromioclavicular cyst secondary to massive rotator cuff tear (fits all imaging and clinical findings, including massive cuff tear, ACJ cyst, subacromial narrowing and humeral head migration).  
2) Acromioclavicular cyst (close but less specificity since no rotator cuff tear).  
3) Degenerative ACJ changes (present but does not explain all findings).  
4) Soft-tissue tumour (less likely due to cystic nature).  
5) ACJ traumatic injury (no trauma history, less likely).  

Hence, the most specific diagnosis is "Acromioclavicular cyst secondary to massive rotator cuff tear".  
</analysis>  
<final>Acromioclavicular cyst secondary to massive rotator cuff tear</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 71-year-old lady reported to the emergency with a history of a fall and was referred for CT brain. Retrospective history revealed recurrent swelling and itching over the left temporal region for the last 6 months.
 Imaging Findings: No trauma-related abnormality was seen on CT. However, a thick-walled cystic lesion was noted in the subcutaneous plane on the left temporal region of the scalp (Figure 1).
 
 Ultrasound of the lesion showed a cyst with actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures, having echogenic walls showing
 
 a “filarial dance”
 
 sign (Figures 2 and 3).
PostDescription:

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient: 71-year-old lady, history of fall but no trauma-related abnormalities on CT.  
- Retrospective history: recurrent swelling and itching over left temporal scalp region for 6 months.  
- Imaging: CT brain shows a thick-walled cystic lesion in the subcutaneous plane of left temporal scalp.  
- Ultrasound: shows cyst with actively mobile, tubular serpiginous structures with echogenic walls, demonstrating "filarial dance" sign.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Dirofilariasis:  
   Pros: Dirofilariasis typically presents as a subcutaneous cystic lesion with mobile echogenic structures (worms) on ultrasound; the "filarial dance" sign is classic for live filarial worms. Patient history of chronic swelling and itching over scalp is consistent.  
   Cons: Dirofilaria is less common in humans, but possible and fits imaging with live worms.  

2) Epidermal inclusion cyst:  
   Pros: Common subcutaneous cystic lesions, usually well-marginated, non-inflammatory unless infected.  
   Cons: No echogenic mobile structures on ultrasound, no "filarial dance" sign. Chronic symptoms typically less inflammatory; no mobile worms expected.  

3) Cysticercosis:  
   Pros: Can show cystic lesions with scolices in subcutaneous tissues; can cause subcutaneous nodules.  
   Cons: Usually shows characteristic "dot in a hole" (scolex) on ultrasound/CT/MRI; live motile serpiginous structures show active movement is not typical; no "filarial dance" sign.  

4) Foreign body granuloma:  
   Pros: Can cause cystic mass with thick wall due to chronic inflammation.  
   Cons: No mobile echogenic tubular structures; no "filarial dance" sign; not typical for itching and swelling over 6 months with mobile worms visible.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Dirofilariasis: Fits classic ultrasound with "filarial dance" sign and clinical scenario of subcutaneous cyst with mobile worms. Pathognomonic.  
2) Cysticercosis: Possible but lacks classic findings; no "filarial dance," usually scolex is not moving.  
3) Epidermal inclusion cyst: No ultrasound signs of mobility or worms; less likely.  
4) Foreign body granuloma: No imaging features of mobile structures; least consistent.  

<final>  
Dirofilariasis  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:

 Imaging Findings: An
 
 orthopantomogram
 
 showed a large exophytic sclerotic bone lesion in the left mandible ramus, persistent primary teeth, caries, and multiple smaller sclerotic bone lesions interspersed in the mandible, some with cortical expansion (Figure 1). A CT scan of the maxillofacial region confirmed the presence of a
 
 dense ivory-like exophytic lesion growing out of the left mandibular ramus
 
 (Figure 2), compatible with an osteoma, and multiple well-delineated lesions of mature bone between the roots of the teeth compatible with hypercementosis.
 
 During clinical examination, a mass was palpated on the forehead leading to a CT scan of the skull, revealing multiple small, sessile sclerotic lesions protruding from the outer table (Figure 3), also consistent with osteomas.

7) Fibrous dysplasia: Can present as ground-glass or sclerotic bone lesions, often unilateral and involves various bones including craniofacial region. OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: We present the case of a 62-year-old man with a distal oesophageal adenocarcinoma treated with neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy who developed rapid swelling in the left preauricular region within hours after Ivor Lewis oesophagectomy. CT was performed to rule out active bleeding and postoperative haematoma.
 Imaging Findings: CT showed diffuse enlargement of the left parotid gland with increased heterogeneous uptake of contrast material, fat stranding in the left parotid space, and thickening of the adjacent superficial layer of the deep cervical fascia (Figures 1 and 3). Oedema and fat stranding extended slightly into the soft tissues of the ipsilateral buccal and masticator spaces, and the sternocleidomastoid muscle was enlarged (Figures 2 and 4). There was no involvement of deeper compartments.
 
 No haematomas or signs of active bleeding were observed.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: We present the case of a 62-year-old man with a distal oesophageal adenocarcinoma treated with neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy who developed rapid swelling in the left preauricular region within hours after Ivor Lewis oesophagectomy.  Imaging Findings: CT showed diffuse enlargement of the left parotid gland with increased heterogeneous uptake of contrast material, fat stranding in the left parotid space, and thickening of the adjacent superficial layer of the deep cervical fascia. Oedema and fat stranding extended slightly into the soft tissues of the ipsilateral buccal and masticator spaces, and the sternocleidomastoid muscle was enlarged. There was no involvement of deeper compartments. No haematomas or signs of active bleeding were observed.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Acute postoperative parotitis, Haematoma and active bleeding, Postsurgical infection (e.g., abscess), Viral parotitis (mumps), Bacterial sialadenitis

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient: 62-year-old man post Ivor Lewis oesophagectomy for distal esophageal adenocarcinoma.  
- Symptom: Rapid swelling in left preauricular region within hours after surgery.  
- CT findings: Diffuse enlargement of left parotid gland, heterogeneous contrast enhancement, fat stranding in left parotid space, thickening of superficial layer of deep cervical fascia adjacent to parotid, mild oedema/fat stranding in ipsilateral buccal and masticator spaces, enlargement of sternocleidomastoid muscle.  
- No haematoma or active bleeding.  
- No deep compartment involvement.

B) Candidate pass/fail with rationale:  
1) Acute postoperative parotitis  
   - Pros: Parotid gland enlargement with heterogeneous enhancement and fat stranding consistent with inflammation; postoperative timing and rapid onset; involvement of adjacent fascia and soft tissues typical of cellulitis/inflammation.  
   - Cons: None significant; no abscess or focal collections seen to suggest complicated infection.  

2) Haematoma and active bleeding  
   - Pros: Postoperative swelling could raise suspicion.  
   - Cons: No haematoma or signs of active bleeding on CT; imaging shows inflammatory changes rather than hyperdense fluid collection typical of hematoma.  

3) Postsurgical infection (e.g., abscess)  
   - Pros: Postoperative setting, soft tissue swelling and edema.  
   - Cons: No discrete fluid collection or rim enhancement seen to suggest abscess; no gas or necrosis; inflammation appears diffuse rather than localized abscess formation.  

4) Viral parotitis (mumps)  
   - Pros: Parotid gland enlargement.  
   - Cons: Unlikely in this postoperative context; no mention of viral prodrome or bilateral involvement; no typical bilateral or non-malignancy-associated features; acute postoperative setting favors bacterial/inflammatory cause rather than viral.  

5) Bacterial sialadenitis  
   - Pros: Parotid enlargement with heterogeneous enhancement and fat stranding typical of bacterial infection; edema of adjacent tissues; timing after surgery; no abscess but early infection plausible.  
   - Cons: No discrete abscess yet, but early stage could be consistent; this is effectively similar to acute postoperative parotitis (inflammatory/infectious cause).  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Acute postoperative parotitis (matches clinical timeline and imaging findings, diffuse inflammatory changes without abscess).  
2) Bacterial sialadenitis (essentially the same entity in this context, but if forced to separate, this would be the infectious subtype of parotitis).  
3) Postsurgical infection (abscess) – less likely due to lack of abscess formation.  
4) Viral parotitis – less likely given context and imaging features.  
5) Haematoma and active bleeding – excluded by imaging.  

Best and most specific diagnosis is "Acute postoperative parotitis."
</analysis><final>Acute postoperative parotitis</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-day-old male presented with non-bilious emesis and a left thigh cutaneous lesion. The patient was found to have pyloric atresia requiring gastroduodenostomy; this procedure led to intestinal malrotation, and the appendix was removed prophylactically. During surgery, the cutaneous lesion was also biopsied. The patient was transiently jaundiced following surgery; this resolved spontaneously, but a liver ultrasound was requested as part of the workup of this.
 Imaging Findings: Liver US showed a normal biliary tree and 2 hypoechoic liver lesions: the larger a 6 mm lesion in segment IVb (lesion 1). Contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS) was performed to further characterise. Three lesions were examined with 4 boluses of ultrasound (Total vol. 1.1mL, Optison, GE Healthcare Inc., using a Phillips EPIQ machine). Lesion 1 had increased in size (seg. IVb, now 32 mm) (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d); Lesions 2 (seg. II, 7 mm) (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) and 3 were also seen (seg. I,10 mm) (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d).
 
 To further characterise, a liver MRI with hepatobiliary contrast was performed. Lesion 1 (34 mm) was T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, infiltrative, rectangular area with capsular retraction (Figures 4a, 4b and 4c). Lesions 2 (Figure 4d) and 3 were T1 hypointense with central T2 hyperintensity relative to the liver. No lesions showed enhancement, macroscopic fat, or diffusion restriction.

- Contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS) with four boluses of Optison.  
- Liver MRI with hepatobiliary contrast: lesion 1 is T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, infiltrative, rectangular, with capsular retraction.  
- Lesions 2 and 3 are T1 hypointense with central T2 hyperintensity relative to liver.

- No abnormal biliary tree.  
- Clinical context includes skin biopsy of a cutaneous lesion and gastrointestinal surgery/post-op jaundice that resolved spontaneously.  
- The presence of a skin lesion and pyloric atresia may suggest a systemic or genetic condition.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 2 pros/con each:  
1. Hepatoblastoma  
- Pros: Common liver tumor in infants; often T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense; can have a large lesion with capsular retraction.  
- Cons: Usually show enhancement on imaging; absence of diffusion restriction and lack of enhancement makes it less likely. Also, usually unilateral or solitary, not multifocal without enhancement.  
2. Lymphoma  
- Pros: Can present as hypovascular lesions with no enhancement.  
- Cons: Rare in neonates; not typical for multiple liver lesions without systemic signs.  
3. Infantile myofibromatosis  
- Pros: Can involve skin and internal organs in infants; skin lesion was biopsied which suggests systemic involvement; myofibromas can appear as T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense lesions; absence of enhancement is typical; multifocal lesions common.  
- Cons: Uncommon for liver involvement, but documented.  
4. Neuroblastoma  
- Pros: Common pediatric tumor; can metastasize to liver.  
- Cons: Usually hypervascular with enhancement; typically presents with abdominal mass rather than small liver lesions; presence of multiple liver lesions without enhancement is atypical.  
5. Haemangiomata  
- Pros: Common benign liver tumors in infants; typically T2 hyperintense and show enhancement.  
- Cons: Absence of enhancement contradicts typical haemangioma imaging; usually show characteristic enhancement. <final> Infantile myofibromatosis </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 56-year-old woman with a known history of retroperitoneal sarcoma, who was receiving radiotherapy, was referred for evaluation of newly palpable lumps in her left breast and left axillary region.
 Imaging Findings: A diagnostic bilateral mammogram (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d; previous mammogram is not available for comparison) shows two circumscribed masses in the left breast upper-outer quadrant.
 
 On the ultrasound of the left breast and axilla (Figures 2a and 2b), there are two adjacent hypo-echoic masses with circumscribed borders in the left breast at 2
 
 o’clock; m
 
 oreover, there is a similar mass in the left axillary region.
 
 The abdominal CT scan with contrast (Figures 3a and 3b) shows a large heterogeneously enhancing mass in the retroperitoneal space with left kidney invasion and aortic wall encasement.
 
 The histopathologic exam reveals highly atypical spindle cells with large pleomorphic hyperchromatic nuclei and high nucleo/cytoplasmic ratio (Figure 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-year-old male presented to the orthopaedic surgeon with complaints of recurrent left shoulder pain. The patient practices combat sports and associates the complaints with a recent fall during training. A shoulder magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was conducted, followed by MRI arthrography (MRA) due to inconsistent findings.
 Imaging Findings: MRI revealed oedematous changes in the inferior glenohumeral joint capsule, with increased signal intensity on the fat-saturated proton-density (PDFS) sequence, suggestive of an
 
 inferior glenohumeral ligament
 
 (
 
 IGHL) lesion (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c).
 
 Additionally, there was an increased signal intensity in the superior labrum, which may indicate a labral lesion.
 
 However, this finding was considered non-specific due to the absence of intra-articular fluid or contrast (Figure 1a).
 
 Subsequent MRA revealed an intrasubstance full-thickness rupture of the anterior band of the IGHL with contrast extravasation (Figures 2a and 2b). This finding was not documented on the previous conventional MRI, which only indicated capsular oedema.
 
 Additionally, the axillary pouch changed from its normal U-shape to a J-shape on fat-saturated T2-weighted sequences (Figures 3a and 3b),
 
 indicating IGHL laxity and loss of its stabilising function
 
 .
 
 MRA also identified a
 
 superior labrum from anterior to posterior (
 
 SLAP) lesion (Figures 2c, 2d, and 3c), which was not clearly visible on MRI due to lack of fluid joint distention.

Clinical History: A 67-year-old female patient diagnosed with a malignant renal mass underwent a CT examination for cancer staging. Her general symptoms were interpreted at that time within the oncological context.
 Imaging Findings: Contrast-enhanced CT examination of the thoracic, abdominal and pelvic regions was performed. Multiple images with tissular densities, mostly located peripherally, were identified in both lung areas. The described images ranged in size from a few millimetres (Figures 1a and 1b) to around 22x15 mm (Figures 2a and 2b), and enhanced during the arterial phase similarly to pulmonary vascular structures (Figures 3 and 4), with feeding vessels from lobar arteries and drainage into lobar veins (Figures 5a, 5b, 6a, and 6b).
PostDescription:

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- A 67-year-old female with known malignant renal mass undergoing cancer staging.
- Contrast-enhanced CT of thorax, abdomen, pelvis.
- Multiple peripherally located tissular density nodules in both lungs.
- Nodules range from a few mm to 22x15 mm.
- Nodules enhance during arterial phase similar to pulmonary vasculature.
- Feeding vessels arise from lobar arteries, drainage into lobar veins.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Hypervascular lung metastases
   - Pros: Patient has malignant renal mass, cancer staging relevant; renal cell carcinoma often causes hypervascular lung metastases.
   - Cons: Typically, metastases do not have feeding arteries and draining veins that behave like vascular structures; nodules are usually solid masses without prominent feeding/draining vessels.

2) Pulmonary sequestration
   - Pros: Anomalous blood supply.
   - Cons: Usually singular, congenital lesion, often located in lung base, with systemic arterial supply (often from aorta), not lobar pulmonary arteries; typically does not enhance like pulmonary vessels; usually cystic or solid mass, not multiple nodules.

3) Bronchocoele
   - Pros: None relevant here.
   - Cons: Congenital bronchial dilation, localized, not multiple peripherally enhancing nodules; no feeding arteries or drainage.

4) Pulmonary varix
   - Pros: Vascular dilation.
   - Cons: Rare, usually a single focal dilation of pulmonary vein; does not present as multiple nodules with feeding arteries; not typical in cancer staging.

5) Pulmonary arterial aneurysm
   - Pros: Vascular dilation.
   - Cons: Typically single or few focal aneurysms, not multiple peripherally distributed nodules; rarely in cancer patients without underlying pulmonary hypertension or vasculitis.

6) Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations (PAVMs)
   - Pros: Multiple nodules with feeder arteries (lobar arteries) and draining veins (lobar veins); enhance like vascular structures on arterial phase; multiple lesions can be present; peripheral lungs typical; can be multiple; enhances similar to pulmonary vessels.
   - Cons: Usually congenital or associated with HHT; however, can be incidental.

7) Aberrant systemic to pulmonary communications
   - Pros: Vascular communication.
   - Cons: Usually a single or few abnormal connections, not multiple nodules; systemic arterial supply (not pulmonary arteries); does not fit multiple small peripherally located nodules with feeding lobar arteries.

C) Ranking:
1) Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations - best match due to multiple peripheral lesions with arterial feeders and venous drainage, enhancement pattern matching pulmonary vessels.
2) Hypervascular lung metastases - plausible given renal cancer, but lack of typical vascular feeding/draining vessels and enhancement pattern.
3) Others less likely.

<Tie-break> The presence of feeding from lobar arteries and drainage into lobar veins with enhancing nodules similar to vasculature is classic for PAVMs. Hypervascular metastases usually lack clear feeding/draining vessels and may not enhance identically to vessels.

</analysis><final> Pulmonary arteriovenous malformations </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old man presented to the emergency department with tibial and fibular fractures and a traumatic brain injury (TBI) after a motor vehicle accident.
 Imaging Findings: Imaging studies obtained after the trauma revealed a comminuted wedge fracture of the tibial diaphysis with mild displacement and angulation, along with an overlapping fibular fracture (Figure 1), and multifocal subarachnoid haemorrhage in the right frontoparietal and left parietal regions on brain CT (Figure 2).
 
 Six months after the initial injury, during which the patient remained bedridden without any new trauma history, shoulder X-rays and CT scans were performed due to complaints of shoulder pain (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). The imaging revealed extensive bilateral heterotopic ossification in the lower glenohumeral joint, indicating mature ossification.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 39-year-old man presented to the emergency department with tibial and fibular fractures and a traumatic brain injury (TBI) after a motor vehicle accident.
 Imaging Findings: Imaging studies obtained after the trauma revealed a comminuted wedge fracture of the tibial diaphysis with mild displacement and angulation, along with an overlapping fibular fracture, and multifocal subarachnoid haemorrhage in the right frontoparietal and left parietal regions on brain CT. Six months after the initial injury, during which the patient remained bedridden without any new trauma history, shoulder X-rays and CT scans were performed due to complaints of shoulder pain. The imaging revealed extensive bilateral heterotopic ossification in the lower glenohumeral joint, indicating mature ossification.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Calcific tendinitis, Neurogenic heterotopic ossification, Myositis ossificans, Vascular, granulomatous or metabolic disease, Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva, Tumoral calcinosis, Sarcoma
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 39-year-old male with tibial and fibular fractures and traumatic brain injury after a motor vehicle accident.  
- On brain CT, multifocal subarachnoid hemorrhage is present.  
- Six months later, patient bedbound with shoulder pain.  
- Imaging shows extensive bilateral heterotopic ossification in the lower glenohumeral joint, indicating mature ossification.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Calcific tendinitis:  
   - Pro: Can cause calcifications near joints.  
   - Con: Usually localized to tendons, not extensive bilateral heterotopic ossification in joints; calcific tendinitis typically does not produce mature bone formation and is not symmetrical or extensive.  

2) Neurogenic heterotopic ossification:  
   - Pro: Strongly associated with traumatic brain injury and prolonged immobilization or spasticity; leads to mature heterotopic bone formation in periarticular soft tissues, often around the hips and shoulders.  
   - Con: None; fits classic presentation.  

3) Myositis ossificans:  
   - Pro: Ossification in muscle after trauma; can mature over weeks to months.  
   - Con: Usually unilateral and localized; not typically bilateral extensive heterotopic ossification in a joint without new trauma history.  

4) Vascular, granulomatous or metabolic disease:  
   - Pro: Can lead to calcification or ossification in tissues.  
   - Con: Not typical to cause extensive mature heterotopic ossification after TBI; no systemic symptoms described.  

5) Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva (FOP):  
   - Pro: Progressive heterotopic ossification.  
   - Con: FOP is genetic, usually presents in childhood/adolescence; often involves cervical spine early; no mention of typical congenital hallux valgus or family history; traumatic brain injury is not typical precipitant.  

6) Tumoral calcinosis:  
   - Pro: Can cause calcified masses in soft tissues.  
   - Con: Usually around large joints, often in younger patients, not mature bone formation; doesn't typically follow TBI.  

7) Sarcoma:  
   - Pro: Can cause bone formation within soft tissues.  
   - Con: Rare; imaging would likely show aggressive/malignant features, not mature, symmetrical ossification; clinical context does not suggest malignancy.  

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Neurogenic heterotopic ossification (pathognomonic for TBI and mature widespread ossification in joints)  
2) Myositis ossificans (localized, usually unilateral, less likely here)  
3) Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva (genetic, chronic, no systemic signs)  
4) Tumoral calcinosis (calcified masses, not mature bone formation)  
5) Calcific tendinitis (localized, not extensive or bilateral per joint)  
6) Vascular, granulomatous or metabolic disease (no systemic signs or lab findings)  
7) Sarcoma (no aggressive features, symmetrical mature ossification)  

Based on the context (traumatic brain injury, prolonged immobilization, mature bilateral heterotopic ossification around glenohumeral joints), the diagnosis that fits best is neurogenic heterotopic ossification.  
</analysis>  
<final>Neurogenic heterotopic ossification</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 81-year-old woman who was presumed to be suffering from polymyalgia rheumatica and was receiving 2.5mg/week of ledertrexate. The patient suffered from chondrocalcinosis of the wrist joints, bilateral arthritis in her hands and repeated bouts of plurifocal synovitis in her wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints. The patient presented for an ultrasound due to Achilles tendon pain due to suspected bilateral Achilles tendonitis.
 Imaging Findings: Multiple hyperechogenic elongated deposits with acoustic shadowing are located within the central part of the right Achilles tendon and following its long axis (Figures 1 and 2), as well as in the left Achilles tendon (Figures 3 and 4). The Achilles tendons are thickened and hypoechogenic. The findings were identical in both Achilles tendons.
PostDescription:

The Achilles tendons are thickened and hypoechogenic. The findings were identical in both Achilles tendons.

1) Intra-tendinous calcifications following trauma
   - Pros: Achilles tendonitis and calcifications could follow trauma.
   - Cons: No history of trauma mentioned; bilateral symmetrical deposits and chondrocalcinosis elsewhere suggest systemic/metabolic cause rather than trauma.

2) Gout related-intra-tendinous calcifications
   - Pros: Gout can cause intra-tendinous tophi, sometimes calcified.
   - Cons: No clinical history or hyperuricemia mentioned; presence of chondrocalcinosis and repeated synovitis suggests CPPD; gout usually shows mixed echogenicity rather than classic acoustic shadowing linear deposits.

3) Idiopathic intra-tendinous calcifications
   - Pros: Could explain unknown cause.
   - Cons: Patient has known chondrocalcinosis and synovitis, making idiopathic less likely; multiple deposits bilateral symmetrical is less typical for idiopathic.

   - Pros: Patient has known chondrocalcinosis (classic for CPPD); ultrasound findings of hyperechogenic linear deposits with acoustic shadowing; bilateral Achilles tendon involvement aligns with deposition disease; thickened, hypoechogenic tendon consistent with tendonitis associated with CPPD.
   - Cons: None significant; fits well clinically and imaging-wise.

5) Hydroxyapatite deposits
   - Pros: Hydroxyapatite can deposit in tendons, causing calcifications.
   - Cons: Normally hydroxyapatite deposits are punctate, non-shadowing; Achilles tendon involvement less common; patient context favors CPPD.

C) Ranking:
1) Calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease (CPPD) - Best fit due to chondrocalcinosis, bilateral symmetric deposits, clinical history and ultrasound findings.
2) Gout related-intra-tendinous calcifications - Possible but less likely given absence of gout symptoms and stronger CPPD evidence.
3) Idiopathic intra-tendinous calcifications - Unlikely due to systemic signs.
4) Hydroxyapatite deposits - Less likely tendon and acoustic shadowing pattern.
5) Intra-tendinous calcifications following trauma - No trauma history; less likely.

</analysis><final>Calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old athlete with chest discomfort and palpitations presented to the cardiologist. Electrocardiogram showed low-density ventricular extrasystoles at rest, which increased in density with stress. Echocardiography revealed mild mitral regurgitation and mild left ventricular hypertrophy
 
 with a basal septal thickness of 12 mm.
 Imaging Findings: Cardiac magnetic resonance was performed on a 3 Tesla MRI scanner. Steady-state free precession (SSFP) sequences identify billowing of both mitral leaflets, with mitral valve prolapse (MVP) of the posterior leaflet (5 mm), mitral annular disjunction (6 mm) and “curling” (Figures 1a and 1b; yellow arrow). Mitral regurgitation was not significant. Biventricular function was normal.
 
 Late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) was present in the subendocardial–intramyocardial layer of the basal inferolateral wall, suggesting replacement fibrosis (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d; white arrows). Native T1 mapping in the same region was elevated: 1360 ms (local normal range 1150–1250 ms), confirming myocardial fibrosis (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d; yellow arrow). Cardiac CT ruled out significant stenosis in the circumflex artery and posterolateral artery (Figure 3; circumflex artery: yellow arrow).

 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound (US), the mass was poorly defined and hyperechoic compared to muscle, located in the subcutaneous tissue. The lesion exhibited a serpentine pattern of hypoechoic bands with a hyperechoic border [1] (Figure 1a).
 
 It showed low vascularity on colour Doppler (Figure 1b).
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a non-encapsulated soft tissue mass in the subcutaneous tissue of the arm, resting on the fascia without involving the underlying muscle. T1 and T1 fat-saturated images showed isointense tissue relative to muscle, with interspersed fatty foci (Figures 2a and 2b). No enhancement was noted after gadolinium injection (Figure 2c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old male, who had a renal transplantation from a cadaveric donor 3 months earlier due to diffuse mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis.
 Imaging Findings: In view of worsening renal function indices and hyperpyrexia, the patient underwent an ultrasound which highlighted grade III hydro-nephrosis. The patient was admitted to our hospital to undergo a unilateral percutaneous nephrostomy
 
 procedure that was performed under fluoroscopy guidance and intravenous deep sedation. Because the graft was in the right iliac fossa
 
 , t
 
 he patient was supine.
 
 The antegrade pyelography revealed a stricture at the ureteral-bladder anastomosis and a
 
 tortuosity of the ureter
 
 , causing an occlusive narrowing of the ureter that prevented the contrast medium from passing into the bladder.
 
 Despite several attempts through multiple angles of the C-arm, from 18° cranial (CRA) to 34° caudal (CAU) and from 52° right (RAO) to 19° left (LAO) angulations, the ureteric bud cannot be visualised
 
 (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d). After a failed attempt to overcome the ureteral narrowing, an 8.5 Fr nephrostomy drainage was placed in the renal pelvis.
 
 The second attempt was scheduled for 2 weeks later.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old male, who had a renal transplantation from a cadaveric donor 3 months earlier due to diffuse mesangial proliferative glomerulonephritis.
 Imaging Findings: In view of worsening renal function indices and hyperpyrexia, the patient underwent an ultrasound which highlighted grade III hydronephrosis. The patient was admitted to our hospital to undergo a unilateral percutaneous nephrostomy procedure that was performed under fluoroscopy guidance and intravenous deep sedation. Because the graft was in the right iliac fossa, the patient was supine. The antegrade pyelography revealed a stricture at the ureteral-bladder anastomosis and a tortuosity of the ureter, causing an occlusive narrowing of the ureter that prevented the contrast medium from passing into the bladder. Despite several attempts through multiple angles of the C-arm, from 18° cranial (CRA) to 34° caudal (CAU) and from 52° right (RAO) to 19° left (LAO) angulations, the ureteric bud cannot be visualised. After a failed attempt to overcome the ureteral narrowing, an 8.5 Fr nephrostomy drainage was placed in the renal pelvis. The second attempt was scheduled for 2 weeks later.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Post-transplant renal artery stent, Post-transplant renal vein stenting, Post-transplant ureteral stent removal, Post-transplant ureteral stent placement

- Ultrasound: grade III hydronephrosis.  
- Fluoroscopy antegrade pyelography: shows stricture at ureteral-bladder anastomosis and tortuosity of the ureter causing occlusive narrowing; contrast does not pass into the bladder; ureteric bud not visualized despite multiple angular attempts.  
- Intervention: attempted to bypass narrowing unsuccessfully; placed 8.5 Fr nephrostomy drainage in renal pelvis; second attempt scheduled later.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Post-transplant renal artery stent - CONTRA: Imaging findings focus on ureteral anatomy and obstruction; no vascular findings described; no mention of artery or stent deployment.  
2) Post-transplant renal vein stenting - CONTRA: Similar to artery stent, no venous findings or symptoms described; imaging focused on urinary tract obstruction.  
3) Post-transplant ureteral stent removal - CONTRA: No mention of any stent being present or removed; findings consistent with obstruction at ureteral-bladder junction; no stent-related complications reported.  
4) Post-transplant ureteral stent placement - PASS: Placement of ureteral stent is typical in transplant ureteral strictures; imaging findings consistent with attempting to resolve ureteral obstruction; nephrostomy placed after failing to bypass narrowing; stent placement is a standard next step to manage strictures.

1) Post-transplant ureteral stent placement (most consistent with imaging and clinical context)  
2) Post-transplant ureteral stent removal (unlikely, no stent noted)  
3) Post-transplant renal vein stenting (no venous findings)  
4) Post-transplant renal artery stent (no arterial findings)  
</analysis>  
<final>Post-transplant ureteral stent placement</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 24-year-old lady presented to the emergency department with accelerated hypertension. She had a history of recurrent episodes of sudden onset palpitations, headache and giddiness for the past two years, with uncontrolled hypertension despite being on multiple antihypertensive medications. Her blood investigations revealed elevated serum normetanephrine (1990 pg/mL).
 Imaging Findings: Trans-abdominal ultrasound (Figures 1a and 1b) showed a well-defined heterogeneously hyperechoic lesion in the right adnexa, seen separate from the right ovary, measuring approximately 4.7 x 5 x 4.8 cm, and with high internal vascularity.
 
 CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis (Figures 2a, 2b, 3a, and 3b) showed a hyperdense mass (average HU of 29) with intense heterogeneous arterial enhancement and few central non-enhancing areas suggestive of necrosis. It was located in the right adnexa and paracervical region, abutting the uterus (which was displaced to the left). It was abutting the posterior urinary bladder wall anteriorly and the rectum posteriorly with no invasion of the right ureter, iliac vessels and lateral pelvic side wall.
 
 On MRI of the pelvis, the lesion was deep to the lateral pelvic fascia on the right, heterogeneously iso to hyperintense on T1WI (Figures 4b and 5), heterogeneously hyperintense on T2WI (
 
 “light bulb sign”
 
 ) (Figure 4a), with areas of restricted diffusion on DWI and maintained fat planes with the adjacent structures (Figures 6a and 6b).
 
 On 68Ga-DOTATATE scan, there was intense homogeneous SSTR (somatostatin receptor) expression in a large well-defined soft tissue density mass in the right adnexa and paracervical region with maximum standardised uptake value (SUV max) of 20.19 (Figure 7).

A) Key findings:

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old woman diagnosed with recurrent meningioma located in the foramen magnum consulted for headache and paraesthesia of the upper limbs. It was decided to perform decompressive surgery and, in the immediate postoperative period, she presented tetraplegia with preservation of consciousness and requirement of mechanical ventilation.
 Imaging Findings: The preoperative MRI showed an extra-axial expansive lesion located in the foramen magnum, measuring 3.9 x 2.1 cm, and producing a mass effect on the bulb and spinal cord, displacing them posteriorly and to the left. It also compromised the V4 segment of the right vertebral artery (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d).
 
 The MRI on day +2 after the surgery showed signs of tumour persistence of the partially removed lesion (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c) and signal alteration in the bulb and upper spinal cord, with hyperintensity in T2/FLAIR sequences (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c) and hypointensity in T1. Diffusion sequences showed signs of mixed oedema (vasogenic-cytotoxic), all in relation to ischemic-oedematous phenomena (Figures 4a to 4i).

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- A 63-year-old woman with recurrent meningioma at the foramen magnum.  
- Pre-operative MRI: extra-axial expansive lesion at the foramen magnum, causing mass effect on medulla and cervical spinal cord, displacing them posteriorly and to the left; involvement of V4 segment of right vertebral artery.  
- Post-operative MRI (day +2): signs of incomplete tumor removal and signal changes in bulb and upper spinal cord: T2/FLAIR hyperintensity and T1 hypointensity.  
- Diffusion-weighted imaging shows mixed edema (vasogenic and cytotoxic), compatible with ischemic-edematous changes.  
- Clinically, post-op tetraplegia with preserved consciousness and need for mechanical ventilation.  
B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Central cord syndrome: Typically from trauma causing disproportionate weakness in upper extremities due to damage to central spinal cord regions. Usually seen in cervical trauma with known mechanism, not here. Pros: Tetraplegia with upper limb involvement. Cons: No traumatic spinal cord injury reported, lesion is extra-axial tumor, not typical for central cord syndrome; imaging shows ischemic changes and tumor involvement, not trauma.  
2) White cord syndrome: Described as ischemic-reperfusion injury after rapid decompression of chronic spinal cord compression, with T2 hyperintensity, mixed edema, neurological deterioration after surgery, without obvious compressive lesions like hematoma or ischemia. Occurs after tumor resection. Pros: Post-op neurological worsening, imaging findings of T2 hyperintensity and mixed edema in cord, no hematoma, consistent with ischemic-reperfusion injury; documented after surgery for foramen magnum meningioma. Cons: Rare syndrome but fits clinical and imaging scenario.  
3) Compressive myelopathy: Chronic compressive lesion causing cord edema and myelopathic symptoms. Pros: Tumor causes pre-op compressive signs; post-op signs of tumor persistence causing continued compression. Cons: Post-op deterioration less likely from compression when surgery performed, plus imaging shows ischemic changes; would require another mass effect which is not described.  
4) Epidural haematoma: Can cause acute spinal cord compression. Pros: Post-op deterioration could be due to bleed. Cons: No imaging evidence of hematoma reported; MRI does not mention epidural blood collection; T2 changes consistent with ischemia, not blood.  

C) Ranking:  
1) White cord syndrome (strong fit with post-op neurological decline after decompression, ischemic cord changes, no hematoma);  
2) Compressive myelopathy (some tumor left but less likely cause of acute deterioration);  
3) Central cord syndrome (less fitting due to no trauma);  
4) Epidural haematoma (excluded by imaging).  
Thus, White cord syndrome fits best as a known complication after rapid decompression of spinal cord with ischemic changes and clinical deterioration.  
</analysis>

OriginalDescription:

CT imaging also demonstrated the marked widening of the intercondylar notch (Figures 4 and 5).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 29-year-old healthy woman presented with a 4-day history of right breast pain and periareolar bruising associated with a 1-day periareolar indurated tumefaction. She denied any other symptoms, trauma, or regular medication use. Physical examination revealed a 2-cm indurated, painful cord-like structure in the periareolar region associated with bruising and petechial lesions.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasonography of the right breast revealed a subcutaneous, non-compressible, tubular hypoechoic structure with low-level internal echoes along an extension of approximately 28 mm (Figures 1 and 2). No blood flow was detected on colour or spectral Doppler studies (Figure 3). Furthermore, the adjacent soft tissues showed increased echogenicity. No other anomalies were seen in the right breast. Ultrasound scans of the left breast and axillary regions yielded no significant findings.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 53-year-old male patient presented with a lump in his left jaw, with noted enlargement in 2 months. There were no associated symptoms or relevant clinical history. Physical examination disclosed an expansile lesion in the left mandibular region and excluded palpable lymph nodes.
 Imaging Findings: Non-enhanced computed tomography (CT) dental scan showed an expansile lytic lesion centred to the angle and ascending ramus of the left mandible, measuring 4 cm at its largest axis, with well-defined, multilobulated borders and ground-glass ossified matrix (Figures 1a and 1b). The final report included osteosarcoma and chondrosarcoma as diagnostic hypotheses and the patient was referred to our tertiary hospital.
 
 Contrast-enhanced cervical CT performed at our hospital better depicted that the lesion did not invade adjacent structures (Figure 2) and enabled evaluation of the infra-hyoid neck. A solid, hypodense, 3.5 cm mass was identified posterior to the right thyroid lobe (Figure 3) and a provisional diagnosis of parathyroid adenoma was proposed.
 
 On ultrasound, the referred nodule was well-defined and hypoechoic (Figures 4a and 4b).
 
 Laboratory findings revealed elevated parathyroid hormone (PTH) (301pg/mL) and elevated calcium levels (13mg/dL).
 
 Parathyroid scintigraphy with technetium-99m methoxy-isobutyl-isonitrile (
 
 Tc-99m MIBI)
 
 was performed and an adenoma of the right inferior parathyroid was confirmed (Figures 5a and 5b).
PostDescription:
