 Imaging Findings: Non-enhanced computed tomography (CT) dental scan showed an expansile lytic lesion centred to the angle and ascending ramus of the left mandible, measuring 4 cm at its largest axis, with well-defined, multilobulated borders and ground-glass ossified matrix. The final report included osteosarcoma and chondrosarcoma as diagnostic hypotheses and the patient was referred to our tertiary hospital. Contrast-enhanced cervical CT performed at our hospital better depicted that the lesion did not invade adjacent structures and enabled evaluation of the infra-hyoid neck. A solid, hypodense, 3.5 cm mass was identified posterior to the right thyroid lobe and a provisional diagnosis of parathyroid adenoma was proposed. On ultrasound, the referred nodule was well-defined and hypoechoic. Laboratory findings revealed elevated parathyroid hormone (PTH) (301pg/mL) and elevated calcium levels (13mg/dL). Parathyroid scintigraphy with technetium-99m methoxy-isobutyl-isonitrile (Tc-99m MIBI) was performed and an adenoma of the right inferior parathyroid was confirmed.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Giant cell tumour, Giant cell reparative granuloma, Fibrous dysplasia, Brown tumour, Ossifying fibroma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old woman with a 10-year history of limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis presented with insidious pain and swelling of the left thumb’s fingertip. Physical examination revealed sclerodactyly and a tender nodule on the distal phalanx of the first finger.
 Imaging Findings: Radiographic imaging of the left hand (Figures 1a and 1b) and thumb (Figure 2) revealed a well-defined calcification in the soft tissue overlying the distal phalanx of the left first finger, measuring approximately 1.5 cm. Periarticular osteopenia was also observed, particularly evident at the thumb (Figure 1b). Cortical thinning and blurring of the margins were noted in the proximal interphalangeal joint of the ring finger (Figure 1a). No other significant findings were identified.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old woman with a 10-year history of limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis presented with insidious pain and swelling of the left thumb’s fingertip. Physical examination revealed sclerodactyly and a tender nodule on the distal phalanx of the first finger.
 Imaging Findings: Radiographic imaging of the left hand and thumb revealed a well-defined calcification in the soft tissue overlying the distal phalanx of the left first finger, measuring approximately 1.5 cm. Periarticular osteopenia was also observed, particularly evident at the thumb. Cortical thinning and blurring of the margins were noted in the proximal interphalangeal joint of the ring finger. No other significant findings were identified.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Calcinosis (in the setting of limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis), Gouty tophi, Tumoural calcinosis, Metastatic calcification, Calcific tendonitis
FinalDiagnosis: Calcinosis (in the setting of limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis)

   - Pros: Soft tissue calcification can occur; pain and swelling; osteopenia may be present.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 58-year-old male with worsening nausea for the past 72 hours presented to the emergency department with severe chest pain radiating to the back in the context of 4 recent episodes of non-bloody, non-bilious emesis. He was afebrile, diaphoretic, and mildly tachycardic. Cardiac workup, complete blood count, and electrolytes were normal.
 Imaging Findings: A chest X-ray followed by contrast-enhanced chest CT were performed. The X-ray demonstrated pneumomediastinum extending into the neck and a tiny left apical pneumothorax. Notably, left pleural effusion with atelectasis and consolidation were also seen (Figure 1a). The coronal CT images were closely correlated to the X-ray findings, clearly showing the pneumomediastinum extending superiorly towards the neck soft tissues. A pneumothorax at the left lower lung base, not previously seen on X-ray, is also demonstrated on coronal CT view (Figure 1b).
 
 The axial chest CT image on lung window showed evidence of pneumomediastinum with air around the oesophagus. Left pneumothorax and bilateral pleural effusions are also seen (Figure 2). On the soft tissue window, a thickened distal oesophagus was seen; however, no clear site of perforation was appreciated. Bilateral complex attenuating pleural effusions were also observed but there was no obvious contrast extravasation (Figure 3).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Case 1 is a 54-year-old female who experienced right ankle pain after an injury, leading to the clinical suspicion of Achilles tendon rupture, and was evaluated by radiograph and MRI after 1 week.
 
 Case 2 is a 62-year-old male who reported calf pain, raising concerns for deep vein thrombosis, further evaluated with ultrasound. He noted a trivial trauma
 
 occurred 10 days prior.
 Imaging Findings: In Case 1, the lateral ankle radiograph revealed increased soft tissue thickness around the Achilles tendon with small calcification (Figure 1). MRI indicated significant disruption (95%) of the distal 5.5 cm of the tendon, cystic areas, and signs of haemorrhage (Figures 2a, 2b, 3a, 3b, and 4). Extensive oedema in the posterior lower leg muscles led to a screening of the leg that identified a 2.8 x 1.6 x 5.4 cm collection in the intermuscular fascial plane, causing indentation on the soleus. The plantaris tendon was partially torn, while the soleus and medial gastrocnemius exhibited grade 1 strain without fibre discontinuity (Figures 5a and 5b).
 
 In Case 2, the ultrasound showed a bulky distal 6.0 cm Achilles tendon with altered echotexture, multiple calcific foci, and a high-grade partial thickness tear (Figures 6a, 6b, and 6c). An anechoic collection measuring 3.3 x 1.2 x 8.6 cm was observed in the intermuscular fascial plane, between the gastrocnemius and soleus muscles, with no increased vascularity noted on the Doppler study (Figures 7a and 7b).

   Pros: Both cases show Achilles tendon discontinuity or partial rupture confirmed by MRI/ultrasound; clinical history and radiographic findings are consistent.

   Cons: No ultrasound evidence of DVT was reported; vascular findings negative; tendon and soft tissue pathology explain pain.

C) Ranking:
1) Achilles rupture with tennis leg - explains both tendon rupture and calf soft tissue collections/edema.
2) Achilles rupture - accounts for tendon injury but not well for calf collections.
3) Tennis leg - cannot explain Achilles tears.
4) Ruptured Bakerâs cyst - not supported by evidence.
5) Deep vein thrombosis - ruled out by imaging.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old male, with skin melanoma (IIIc), on adjuvant immunotherapy with nivolumab since 6 months,
 
 previously without comorbidities,
 
 presented with >5-fold increase in serum lipase and
 
 an onset of diabetes
 
 . Nivolumab was suspended till lipase decreased. After the reintroduction of nivolumab, another elevation of lipase
 
 and abdominal pain
 
 were
 
 noted.
 Imaging Findings: Computed tomography showed diffuse pancreatic parenchymal enlargement, heterogeneity (Figure 1), and slightly increased peripancreatic fat attenuation. There were no radiological signs of cholelithiasis or cholecystitis. Additionally, diffuse colon and rectal wall thickening and hyperenhancement were reported (Figure 2), as well as a triangular enhancement defect in the left kidney (Figure 3).
 
 At the second onset of symptoms, MRI was performed, demonstrating pancreatic volume increase, with signal alterations on T2-weighted images, especially in the pancreatic head, and diffuse restricted diffusion (Figures 4a, 4b, 5, 6a, and 6b). Additionally, mildly expressed peripancreatic and mesenteric fat stranding was noted. No pancreatic duct or biliary tree dilatation was observed. There were no fluid collections, free abdominal fluid or adenopathy.
PostDescription:
Clinical History: A 55-year-old male, with skin melanoma (IIIc), on adjuvant immunotherapy with nivolumab since 6 months, previously without comorbidities, presented with >5-fold increase in serum lipase and an onset of diabetes.  Imaging Findings: Computed tomography showed diffuse pancreatic parenchymal enlargement, heterogeneity, and slightly increased peripancreatic fat attenuation. There were no radiological signs of cholelithiasis or cholecystitis. Additionally, diffuse colon and rectal wall thickening and hyperenhancement were reported, as well as a triangular enhancement defect in the left kidney. At the second onset of symptoms, MRI was performed, demonstrating pancreatic volume increase, with signal alterations on T2-weighted images, especially in the pancreatic head, and diffuse restricted diffusion. Additionally, mildly expressed peripancreatic and mesenteric fat stranding was noted. No pancreatic duct or biliary tree dilatation was observed. model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 55-year-old male with history of stage IIIc skin melanoma on nivolumab immunotherapy
- Laboratory: >5-fold increase in serum lipase, new onset diabetes
- Temporal relation: lipase increase and abdominal pain after nivolumab reintroduction
- CT: diffuse pancreatic parenchymal enlargement, heterogeneity, mildly increased peripancreatic fat attenuation, no cholelithiasis or cholecystitis
- MRI: pancreatic volume increase, T2 signal alterations especially in head, diffuse restricted diffusion, mild peripancreatic and mesenteric fat stranding, no duct or biliary dilatation
- Additional findings: diffuse colon and rectal wall thickening and hyperenhancement; triangular defect in left kidney enhancement
- No fluid collections, no adenopathy

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Viral pancreatitis
   - Pros: Can cause acute pancreatitis with elevated lipase, pancreatic enlargement and fat stranding.
   - Cons: No history or data supporting viral infection; no mention of viral prodrome; the temporal association with nivolumab and multiple organ involvement (colon thickening, renal defect) less typical; no ductal or biliary involvement; diabetic onset and recurrent elevation with nivolumab are less typical.

2) Autoimmune pancreatitis
   - Pros: Diffuse pancreatic swelling and enhancement, T2 signal changes, restricted diffusion all support AIP; colon and rectal wall thickening and enhancement can be seen in IgG4-related disease; triangular kidney defect can be seen in IgG4-related tubulointerstitial nephritis.
   - Cons: No mention of serum IgG4 or other IgG4-related findings, but imaging findings are quite characteristic of AIP and IgG4-related organ involvement; lack of IgG4 levels is a limitation but not a negative.

3) Idiopathic pancreatitis
   - Pros: Elevated lipase, pancreatic enlargement can be nonspecific.
   - Cons: Idiopathic would not explain concurrent colon and rectum changes or renal involvement; recurrent pancreatitis temporally related to nivolumab; no gallstones or alcohol history mentioned; idiopathic diagnosis is a diagnosis of exclusion; here there are multiple systemic signs pointing elsewhere.

4) Immune-related pancreatitis
   - Pros: Patient on nivolumab with recurrent pancreatitis episodes coinciding with immunotherapy; immune-related pancreatitis is a known adverse effect; systemic immune-related colitis (colon and rectal thickening and hyperenhancement) and kidney defect support systemic immune activation; imaging findings consistent with autoimmune pancreatitis; onset of diabetes supports pancreatic endocrine damage due to immune-related inflammation.
   - Cons: This diagnosis overlaps strongly with autoimmune pancreatitis; however, immune-related pancreatitis is a broader clinical syndrome including systemic immune activation.

5) Chronic pancreatitis
   - Pros: Pancreatic enlargement can occur.
   - Cons: Typically patients have history of multiple episodes, calcifications, ductal changes; no mention of duct or biliary dilatation, calcifications, or chronic changes; acute enzyme elevation, new onset diabetes, and systemic organ involvement make this less likely.

6) Normal response to treatment with immune checkpoint inhibitors
   - Pros: Immune therapy can cause mild inflammation.
   - Cons: Serum lipase >5 fold elevation, abdominal pain, pancreatic enlargement, systemic organ involvement (colon, rectum, kidney) and diabetes onset are pathological, not normal response.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:
- 2) Autoimmune pancreatitis is a strong candidate based on imaging (diffuse pancreatic swelling, T2 changes, restricted diffusion), colon and rectal involvement, triangular kidney defect consistent with IgG4-related disease, but lacks IgG4 serology.
- 4) Immune-related pancreatitis best fits the clinical context (nivolumab therapy, temporal relation, systemic organ involvement including colon and kidney), and imaging is consistent with autoimmune pancreatitis which can be the organ manifestation of systemic immune-related adverse events.
- 1) Viral pancreatitis is less likely due to lack of viral signs and systemic involvement.
- 3) Idiopathic pancreatitis is less likely given systemic immune-related findings.
- 5) Chronic pancreatitis is less likely without chronic changes.
- 6) Normal response is untenable given severe clinical findings.

Therefore, the best diagnosis is "Immune-related pancreatitis," as it encompasses both pancreatic involvement and systemic immune-related organ inflammation consistent with immunotherapy-induced adverse effects, and aligns with the clinical presentation and imaging findings.

</analysis>

<final>Immune-related pancreatitis</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old male patient on anticoagulation therapy, with a history of hip fracture and acute renal insufficiency, underwent a standard chest X-ray as part of the preoperative evaluation for a total hip replacement. He was asymptomatic on the respiratory level, with no dyspnoea or chest pain.
 Imaging Findings: The initial X-ray revealed a likely pleural right peripheral opacity with smooth borders and no acute angles (Figure 1).
 
 A CT scan was then performed, demonstrating an extra-pleural collection measuring 30 × 72 × 87 mm (AP × TR × CC). The collection was spontaneously hyperdense, suggesting the presence of blood or fluid with a high protein content. Additionally, the “extra-pleural fat sign” was observed, confirming an extra-pleural process rather than a pleural or lung lesion (Figures 2 and 3).
 
 In the presence of renal insufficiency and an extra-pleural mass, contrast injection was not performed. Consequently, a mass with potential bleeding could not be definitively ruled out, and an MRI was conducted. T1-weighted MRI images showed the extra-pleural collection appearing hyperintense due to the presence of blood products (Figure 4). In T2-weighted images, the lesion was mildly hyperintense, consistent with the presence of subacute haemorrhage or an organised haematoma (Figure 5).
 
 On diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI), a hyperintense signal with ADC restriction was observed (Figures 6a and 6b). After gadolinium injection, a peripheral enhancement of the lesion’s capsule was observed, with no enhancement observed within the lesion itself (Figure 7).
 
 This supports the primary haemorrhagic nature of the lesion rather than an inflammatory or neoplastic process.
 
 Non-contrast CT and MRI scans of the thorax were performed using T1, T2, DWI, and T1 fat-saturated gadolinium sequences in a Philips Ingenia 1.5T high-field MRI scanner and a Philips Incisive 32-slice CT scanner.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 64-year-old man with a prior history of pulmonary tuberculosis presents with an acute onset of significant haemoptysis. He mentions experiencing weight loss, tiredness, and intermittent fever. His medical history is unremarkable, except for tuberculosis, which was treated a decade ago. A chest X-ray performed recently demonstrated cavitary abnormalities.
 Imaging Findings: CT angiography provided the main findings, consisting of a localised aneurysm of a pulmonary artery branch located near a tuberculous cavity (Figures 2a and 2b), representing the aneurysm. Hyperdense opacity with surrounding haematoma indicates active bleeding, while multiple air locules within the opacity are characteristic of a tuberculous cavity (Figures 1a and 1b), distinguishing it from other pulmonary lesions. Centrilobular ground-glass nodules may be observed around the haematoma, suggesting endobronchial dissemination of blood (Figure 4). Post-contrast imaging reveals focal enhancement of the aneurysm, aiding in precise assessment of its size, shape, and location (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c).

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 64-year-old man with prior pulmonary tuberculosis (treated 10 years ago).  
- Acute significant haemoptysis, weight loss, tiredness, intermittent fever.  
- Chest X-ray: cavitary abnormalities.  
- CT angiography: localized aneurysm of a pulmonary artery branch adjacent to a tuberculous cavity.  
- Hyperdense opacity with surrounding hematoma indicating active bleeding.  
- Multiple air locules within opacity consistent with tuberculous cavity.  
- Centrilobular ground-glass nodules around hematoma suggesting blood spread in airways.  
- Post-contrast focal enhancement of aneurysm.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Behçet’s disease: Typically involves systemic vasculitis with possible pulmonary artery aneurysms, but less commonly associated with tuberculous cavities or a history of healed TB. No mention of systemic symptoms typical for Behçet’s. The presence of tuberculous cavity strongly suggests a tuberculous etiology rather than Behçet's autoimmune vasculitis.  
   Pros: Pulmonary artery aneurysm possible.  
   Cons: No history of Behçet’s or systemic findings; presence of TB cavity favors infectious etiology.

2) Pulmonary artery pseudoaneurysm: Could be secondary to infection or trauma. However, the term pseudoaneurysm is non-specific and does not incorporate the typical association with tuberculous cavity.  
   Pros: Aneurysm formation; pseudoaneurysm includes non-true aneurysms.  
   Cons: Does not specify the classic association with tuberculous cavities; diagnosis less precise than Rasmussen aneurysm.

3) Mycotic aneurysm: Infectious aneurysms caused by bacterial, fungal, or septic emboli. Typically involve arterial walls infected by microorganisms. While TB is infectious, mycotic aneurysms usually refer to bacterial/fungal infection, and the classic radiologic and clinical context is different (septic emboli, no cavitary lesion adjacent).  
   Pros: Infectious aneurysm possible.  
   Cons: Rare in pulmonary arteries; no mention of septic emboli or systemic sepsis; TB cavity adjacent to aneurysm is more characteristic of Rasmussen aneurysm.

4) Rasmussen aneurysm: Classic complication of pulmonary tuberculosis, characterized by a pulmonary artery aneurysm adjacent to or within a tuberculous cavity. Typically presents with haemoptysis, history of TB, and imaging shows aneurysm adjacent to cavitary lesion.  
   Pros: History of TB, cystic cavitary lesion, pulmonary artery branch aneurysm adjacent to TB cavity, haemoptysis.  
   Cons: None significant; fits perfectly with classic description.

5) Neoplasm: Could cause cavitary lesions and aneurysm formation if vascular invasion occurs, but no mention of mass lesion, nodules, or enhancement suggestive of tumor. Haemoptysis can occur but the history of TB and cavity with aneurysm points away from malignancy.  
   Pros: Haemoptysis possible, cavitary lesion could be tumor-related.  
   Cons: No mass lesion; aneurysm adjacent to TB cavity more consistent with infection-related aneurysm; no solid nodules with characteristic tumor enhancement.

6) Aspergilloma: Usually appears as a fungus ball within a pre-existing cavity, often seen as air-fluid level with fungal ball density. Can cause haemoptysis but does not typically cause aneurysm. No mention of fungus ball or specific enhancement; presence of aneurysm excludes this.  
   Pros: Cavitary lesion, haemoptysis.  
   Cons: No fungus ball, no specific imaging for aspergilloma; aneurysm formation is not a feature.

C) Ranking:  
1) Rasmussen aneurysm: Pathognomonic association of pulmonary artery aneurysm with tuberculous cavity in prior TB, active bleeding, haemoptysis.  
2) Pulmonary artery pseudoaneurysm: General term, less specific.  
3) Behçet’s disease: No systemic features; less likely given TB history.  
4) Mycotic aneurysm: Infection-related but classic description fits Rasmussen more.  
5) Neoplasm: No mass lesion or tumor features.  
6) Aspergilloma: No fungus ball or pathognomonic signs; aneurysm is not typical.  
Tie-break favors Rasmussen aneurysm due to classic presentation (TB history, cavitary lesion, adjacent pulmonary artery aneurysm, haemoptysis).  
</analysis>  
<final>Rasmussen aneurysm</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-year-old female was admitted to the emergency department due to a 2-week history of constipation, drooling, and left eye strabismus due to oculomotor nerve paralysis. Further physical examination without clinically significant findings. Initial laboratory tests upon admission showed white blood count (WBC), neutrophils, and lymphocytes within normal ranges.
 Imaging Findings: An initial contrast-enhanced CT scan of the brain (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d) showed a lesion that extended to cranial structures and temporal lobes, involving deep neck spaces.
 
 An initial contrast-enhanced thoracoabdominal CT scan (Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) showed pleural thickening, subpleural nodules, pancreatic lesions with dilated pancreatic duct calibre, bilateral renal cortical lesions, and a right paraspinal lesion invading the spinal canal at L2–L3 with nerve roots involvement, suggestive of metastasis. Pelvic lymphadenopathies were identified.
 
 A 2-month follow-up contrast-enhanced MRI (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d) revealed a smaller lesion compared to the previous CT, with decreased intracranial and cerebral extension and a reduced cerebral and cerebellar volume.
 
 The 2-month follow-up contrast-enhanced thoracoabdominal CT scan (Figures 4a, 4b, 4c, and 4d) showed no pleural thickening areas or subpleural nodules. The pancreas with a size reduction of the focal lesions. The pancreatic duct calibre was preserved, and the kidneys had no infiltrative lesions. Generalised reduction of pelvic lymphadenopathy. The paraspinal lesion was not visible. A not previously identified generalised fatty infiltration was shown.

A) Key findings:  
- 2-year-old female presenting with neurological signs (strabismus, oculomotor nerve paralysis).  
- Initial imaging shows a lesion extending to deep neck spaces and temporal lobes, suggesting central nervous system involvement with possible invasion or metastasis.  
- Thoracoabdominal CT shows extensive disease: pleural thickening, subpleural nodules, pancreatic lesions with dilated pancreatic duct, bilateral renal cortical lesions, paraspinal lesion invading spinal canal with nerve root involvement, and pelvic lymphadenopathies.  
- Follow-up imaging after 2 months shows regression of many lesions (lesion size reduction, disappearance of pleural thickening, normalization of pancreatic lesions, resolution of renal lesions, shrinking lymphadenopathy, no paraspinal mass), but new generalized fatty infiltration.  
- Laboratory tests initially normal (no leukocytosis).  
- Rapid and extensive involvement of multiple organ systems, including CNS, pancreas, kidneys, lungs (pleura), lymph nodes, and spine, with subsequent partial regression without clear treatment described.  
  
B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Metastatic pro-B type acute lymphoblastic leukaemia (ALL)  
   Pros:  - Age fits typical ALL patient (young children).  
   - Widespread disease involving blood-rich soft tissues including CNS, lymphatic system, pancreas, and kidneys fits disseminated leukemic infiltration.  
   - Response to treatment plausible (partial regression expected with chemotherapy), explaining lesion shrinkage on follow-up.  
   Cons:  - No mention of hematologic abnormalities at presentation, which would be unusual for ALL but could be in early or treated phases.  
2) Lymphoma  
   Pros:  - Lymphoma can cause multi-organ involvement and can present in young children, especially extranodal forms.  
   - Lymphadenopathy and lesions in multiple organs support lymphoma.  
   Cons:  - CNS involvement as described is less typical.  
   - Rapid regression over 2 months is atypical for untreated lymphoma; usually requires treatment.  
3) Primary visceral organ malignancies  
   Pros:  - Pancreatic lesions and renal lesions could be primary tumors.  
   Cons:  - Extremely rare to have synchronous primary visceral malignancies in a child with such widespread disease and CNS involvement.  
   - The widespread and simultaneous involvement of multiple unrelated organs is highly unlikely for primary visceral cancers.  
4) Sarcoidosis  
   Pros:  - Can cause granulomatous lesions in multiple organs, including lungs, lymph nodes, CNS.  
   Cons:  - Extremely rare in 2-year-old children.  
   - Pancreatic involvement and nerve root invasion less typical.  
   - Rapid lesion regression is not characteristic without therapy; also, sarcoidosis typically shows more indolent course.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Metastatic pro-B type acute lymphoblastic leukaemia: Most fitting given age, multisystem involvement including CNS, rapid response to therapy implied by lesion regression, and typical hematopoietic spread.  
2) Lymphoma: Could explain many findings, but CNS and pancreatic involvement, and rapid regression without clear therapy less typical.  
3) Sarcoidosis: Very unlikely in this age group and with the pattern of disease described.  
4) Primary visceral organ malignancies: Least likely due to rarity in this age and pattern of spread.  

Thus, the diagnosis that best fits all imaging, clinical features and rapid response is Metastatic pro-B type acute lymphoblastic leukaemia.  
</analysis>  
<final>Metastatic pro-B type acute lymphoblastic leukaemia</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: In an ambulatory appointment, a 29-year-old female patient presented with chronic and persistent cough. No smoking history was referred, and the physical examination was unremarkable. An ambulatory chest x-ray was performed in our institution and further evaluation with computed tomography (CT), followed by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), was later requested.
 Imaging Findings: The chest x-ray (Figures 1a and 1b) revealed an opacity, with rounded and well-defined borders, localised in the posterior and medial regions of the right middle lung field.
 
 Contrast-enhanced thoracic CT /Figures 2a, 2b, 2c, and 2d) revealed in the right paravertebral region, next to the posteromedial aspect of the 7
 
 th
 
 and 8
 
 costal arches, a large nodular lesion, with 47 x 49 x 41 mm (L x AP x T), ovoid with regular and well-defined borders, heterogeneous and with marked peripheral contrast uptake. It is localised in an extrapulmonary topography
 
 , as it is centred on the chest wall and shows obtuse angles with the thoracic wall, and it is near proximity to
 
 nerve structures. There were no signs of invasion of adjacent structures, particularly bone structures.
 
 The contrast-enhanced thoracic MRI images (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 3e) confirmed the localisation and nature of the mass, further characterising it as a soft tissue lesion showing heterogeneity of signal in the T2-weighted sequences, with areas of interstitial hyperintensity and markedly heterogeneous uptake after contrast administration.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 46-year-old male patient presented to our department for routine follow-up imaging after left radical orchiectomy 19 years earlier due to a testicular germ cell tumour, and with retroperitoneal recurrence 11 years after surgery. He had a previous history of left orchidopexy during childhood to repair cryptorchidism.
 Imaging Findings: In this patient, who underwent a left radical orchiectomy for a testicular germ cell tumour, the abdominal-pelvic computed tomography (CT) revealed tubular-shaped splenic tissue along the left paracolic gutter, representing a retroperitoneal splenic cord connecting the spleen to the remaining mesonephric structure in the left lower quadrant (Figures 1a to 1i). Additionally, this condition was associated with congenital absence of lower limbs (Figure 2).
 
 Retrospectively, the described abdominal features were considered stable compared to the oldest examination available in our database, which was performed at the time of the retroperitoneal recurrence (Figures 3a to 3f).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 3-year-old female with poor feeding toleration, aspirating all consistencies of food, with failure to thrive, was planned for gastrostomy tube placement, and an upper gastrointestinal study was requested for the anatomic assessment.
 Imaging Findings: A fluoroscopic upper gastrointestinal study was done by injection of barium via the indwelling enteric tube. The stomach exhibited a normal appearance and was normally emptied through a normal pylorus, duodenal bulb, and descending duodenum (Figure 1). After an initial descent of the duodenum, the third part of the duodenum did not cross the midline as expected. Instead, it ascends on the right side of the spine posterior and parallel to the descending duodenum up to the level of the duodenal bulb and then completes its transverse course and reaches the left upper abdomen (Figures 2 and 3).
PostDescription:

DifferentialDiagnosisList: Duodenum inversum, Redundant duodenum, Intestinal malrotation
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 3-year-old female with aspiration and failure to thrive.  
- Upper GI study with barium via feeding tube.  
- Normal stomach and pylorus, normally emptied.  
- Duodenal bulb and descending duodenum normal.  
- The third part of the duodenum does NOT cross the midline. Instead, it ascends on the right side of the spine, posterior and parallel to the descending duodenum, up to the level of the duodenal bulb, then completes its transverse course to the left upper abdomen.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Duodenum inversum:  
- Pros: Duodenum inversum is a rare congenital anomaly where the third portion of duodenum runs superiorly in the right upper quadrant before crossing the midline in an abnormal fashion. This description fits with the third part of the duodenum ascending on the right side instead of crossing the midline in a normal course.  
- Cons: None significant; the described unusual course of the third part matches duodenum inversum.  
  
2) Redundant duodenum:  
- Pros: Can cause looping or kinking of duodenum, sometimes with unusual configuration.  
- Cons: Redundant duodenum typically involves an elongated, tortuous duodenum but does not generally involve the third part ascending on the right side - the abnormal course described is more typical of duodenum inversum. No mention of kinking or looping consistent with redundancy.  
  
3) Intestinal malrotation:  
- Pros: Can cause abnormal positioning of duodenum and other intestines.  
- Cons: Malrotation would typically cause the duodenum to be fixed abnormally, often with a position of the duodenojejunal junction abnormal, and corkscrew appearance of small bowel. Here, the stomach and duodenum up to the third part are normal, and only the third part courses anomalously on the right side, which is not classic for malrotation. Also, malrotation commonly presents with volvulus risk, not specifically with aspirating all consistencies of food and failure to thrive related to duodenal anomaly.  
  
C) Ranking with tie-breaks:  
1) Duodenum inversum – fits the described abnormal course of the third part of the duodenum most closely.  
2) Intestinal malrotation – less likely given the normal location of other duodenal parts and lack of typical malrotation signs.  
3) Redundant duodenum – less likely given no mention of looping or redundancy.  
The pathognomonic feature here is the third part of the duodenum ascending on the right side before crossing the midline, which is classic for duodenum inversum.  

</analysis>  
<final>Duodenum inversum</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 50-year-old female patient presented with headache, visual disturbances, and bitemporal hemianopia. Hormonal assays indicated abnormal levels of serum prolactin and growth hormone.
 Imaging Findings: The patient’s MRI brain showed a large lobulated sellar lesion (Figure 1) on post-contrast sagittal images, showing suprasellar extension and anteriorly extending into both sphenoid sinuses and ethmoid sinuses. Axial T2 image (Figure 2) shows the lesion to be predominantly involving the left cavernous sinus and encasing the left internal carotid artery, and seen extending into the left orbital apex and left frontal and temporal lobes. FLAIR coronal image (Figure 3) shows compression of adjacent brain parenchyma, lateral ventricle, and brainstem. In the post-contrast axial image (Figure 4), the lesion was seen extending into the right cavernous sinus with suprasellar extension into both frontal lobes and sphenoid sinuses.
PostDescription:

Axial T2 image shows the lesion to be predominantly involving the left cavernous sinus and encasing the left internal carotid artery, and seen extending into the left orbital apex and left frontal and temporal lobes. FLAIR coronal image shows compression of adjacent brain parenchyma, lateral ventricle, and brainstem. In the post-contrast axial image, the lesion was seen extending into the right cavernous sinus with suprasellar extension into both frontal lobes and sphenoid sinuses.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Meningioma, Pituitary macroadenoma, Chordoma

A) Key Findings:  
- Age and clinical presentation: 50-year-old female with headache, visual disturbances, bitemporal hemianopia, and abnormal serum prolactin and growth hormone levels.  
- MRI findings:  
  - Large lobulated sellar lesion with suprasellar extension, anterior extension into both sphenoid and ethmoid sinuses.  
  - Involvement of left cavernous sinus and encasement of the left internal carotid artery; extension into left orbital apex, frontal and temporal lobes.  
  - Compression of adjacent brain parenchyma, lateral ventricles, and brainstem.  
  - Extension into right cavernous sinus, with suprasellar extension into both frontal lobes and sphenoid sinuses.  
- Hormonal abnormalities correlate with pituitary hormone excess.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Meningioma  
   - Pros: Can extend into cavernous sinus and adjacent structures; can cause bone involvement and sellar region mass; can encase ICA.  
   - Cons: Typically dural-based, extra-axial lesion; less likely to cause endocrine abnormalities like elevated prolactin or GH; usually does not arise from pituitary gland; lobulated sellar mass with pituitary hormone excess is less typical.  
2) Pituitary macroadenoma  
   - Pros: Classic sellar and suprasellar lesion with extension into sphenoid and ethmoid sinuses; can invade cavernous sinus and encase ICA; endocrine abnormalities with elevated prolactin and GH are hallmark features; causes visual disturbances and bitemporal hemianopia due to optic chiasm compression.  
   - Cons: Extension into temporal lobes and frontal lobes is less common, but aggressive macroadenomas can invade locally; lobulated shape is consistent with macroadenoma.  
3) Chordoma  
   - Pros: Can arise in clival region, invades bone and soft tissues, can extend to sphenoid sinuses.  
   - Cons: Usually midline clival lesion, less likely to present with pituitary hormone abnormalities; typically does not cause prolactin or GH excess; involvement of pituitary gland is uncommon; clinical picture and endocrine findings less consistent.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Pituitary macroadenoma: Fits location (sellar/lobulated lesion), hormonal excess pattern, local invasion pattern including cavernous sinus, ICA encasement, suprasellar and sinus extension; explains visual field deficit.  
2) Meningioma: Possible but endocrine findings and origin less consistent with pituitary adenoma.  
3) Chordoma: Least likely given endocrine abnormalities and primary site.  

<final> Pituitary macroadenoma </final><|return|>
options: Meningioma | Pituitary macroadenoma | Chordoma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Five days after an uncomplicated embolisation of an asymptomatic, growing right middle cerebral artery (MCA) aneurysm, a 56-year-old woman comes to the ER due to headache, a seizure and fluctuant left upper extremity weakness and left campimetric defects. A Todd’s paralysis is suspected, and the patient is admitted for a workup.
 Imaging Findings: Initially, a brain CT is performed, which shows no significant abnormalities apart from the embolisation material (Figure 1).
 
 Then a brain MRI is performed, including the following sequences: DWI (Figures 2a and 2b), SWI (Figure 3), T2, FLAIR (Figure 4), 3D TOF and pre and post-gadolinium T1 (Figures 5a and 5b). The MRI discloses multiple round lesions varying in size in the right hemisphere. The lesions are spontaneously T1 hypointense and T2 hyperintense, the largest ones showing a small, central T2 hypointensity. Post-gadolinium enhancement is demonstrated in all the lesions. No restricted diffusion or susceptibility artefacts are present.
 
 Follow-up imaging after 6 months (Figures 6, 7, 8a, and 8b) shows overall improvement with resolution of the enhancing foci. However, T2 hyperintensity is still present, and central punctate susceptibility artefacts have appeared, interpreted as microhaemorrhagic foci.

   - Pros: Lesions are enhancing but not typically restricted diffusion; no acute infarct signs.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 75-year-old female presented with a four-day history of sudden onset frontal headache, vomiting, ophthalmalgia, photophobia and blurred vision. Her past medical history included migraines, right-sided phacoemulsion and an intraocular lens implant six years ago. Fundoscopy demonstrated choroidal folds and effusions. Laboratory blood workup revealed only elevated serum ACE levels.
 Imaging Findings: Initial computerised tomography (CT) head imaging and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the head were unremarkable. Specifically, CT did not identify any orbital pathology. MRI of the orbits revealed bilateral effusions in the suprachoroidal space, with low signal intensity on T1 sequences (Figure 1) and high-intensity signal on T2 sequences (Figure 2). Findings were more pronounced on the right side. Effusions extended anteriorly past the ora serrata. Contrast-enhanced sequences with fat suppression visualised choroidal and scleral enhancement (Figure 3). There was no evidence of intraocular mass or retinal detachment.
 
 A subsequent high-resolution CT chest scan revealed biapical scarring, mild bronchial wall thickening and subtle fissure nodularity in the upper zones (Figure 4) and a 10 mm subcarinal lymph node (Figure 5). Thoracic imaging findings were non-specific and equivocal for sarcoidosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 13-year-old boy presented with pain over the left hip for the last 3 years and a gradually enlarging mass for the last 2 years. Upon physical examination, the left hip range of motion was reduced. Lab tests showed normal calcium, high serum phosphorous of 7.07 Eq/dL, and normal parathormone levels.
 Imaging Findings: A radiograph of the left hip demonstrated multilobulated periarticular calcifications (Figure 1). MRI (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 3e) showed a well-defined, lobulated lesion affecting the glutei, vastus lateralis, and obturator externus muscles. Multiple fluid-calcium levels, termed the “sedimentation sign”, were noted, with calcifications appearing hypointense on both T1 and T2-weighted sequences, and fluid appearing hypointense on T1-weighted and hyperintense on T2-weighted sequences. These lesions exhibited heterogeneous contrast enhancement. CT imaging revealed a multilobulated calcific mass without bone or joint involvement (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Histopathological examination (Figures 4a and 4b) confirmed the diagnosis of tumoral calcinosis, revealing fibro-collagenous tissue with granular calcifications arranged in lobules with surrounding multinucleated giant cells, foamy macrophages, lymphocytes, and plasma cells.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 73-year-old woman presented with abdominal pain, mild jaundice and dark urine for 3 days. Initial blood tests revealed anaemia and elevation of serum creatinine, total bilirubin, and lactate dehydrogenase. Further analytical evaluation excluded autoimmune and microangiopathic haemolytic anaemias. Flow cytometry analysis of peripheral blood showed a deficiency of GPI-anchored proteins. Abdominal ultrasound did not reveal any significant findings, so an abdominal magnetic resonance (MRI) was subsequently conducted.
 Imaging Findings: MRI excluded acute biliary pathology but revealed abnormal bilateral renal cortical hypointensity on both T2 (Figure 1) and T1-weighted (Figure 2) sequences. In gradient-recalled echo (GRE) imaging, the renal cortex demonstrated a relative increase in signal intensity on the out-of-phase sequence (Figure 3) compared to the in-phase sequence (Figure 2). Reduced cortical enhancement was observed after intravenous contrast administration (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c). The spleen and liver showed no significant parenchymal signal abnormalities (Figure 5). Isolated renal haemosiderosis was presumed.
 
 Abdominal ultrasound and computed tomography did not reveal significant abnormalities.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old male with a history of glioblastoma, submitted to surgical resection followed by Stupp protocol 11 years ago, and organic psychosis, with therapeutic noncompliance and multiple hospitalisations in recent years, presented to the emergency department with acute onset gait disturbance, right-sided muscle weakness, numbness and behavioural changes.
 Imaging Findings: A head CT performed upon admission revealed a status post left parietal craniotomy, with a cortico-subcortical temporoparietal surgical site exhibiting gliosis, stable when compared to previous imaging. No new hypodensities suggestive of stroke were observed, and CT angiography excluded arterial occlusion.
 
 Subsequent brain MRI T1-weighted post-contrast images demonstrated
 
 de novo
 
 cortical gyriform enhancement in the previously treated surgical site, specifically in the temporoparietal region, with extension into the occipital, insular and remaining temporal lobe. Perfusion arterial spin labelling (ASL) study indicated increased cerebral blood flow (CBF) in the affected area. Importantly, there were no signs of acute stroke nor tumour progression, as evidenced by the absence of restricted diffusion, an extension of the high T2 FLAIR signal or nodular enhancement.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 26-year-old female with no relevant past medical history presented with a two-year history of worsening pain at the right proximal humerus and associated weakness.
 Imaging Findings: X-ray of the right shoulder (Figures 1a and 1b) showed an expansile lucent proximal humeral meta-diaphysis lesion with well-defined medial and craniocaudal margins, narrow zone of transition, mild lateral endosteal scalloping and subtle medial periosteal reaction.
 
 CT of the right shoulder (Figures 2a and 2b) showed a predominantly fat-density soft tissue mass and an extensive area of full-thickness cortical loss anteriorly with focal permeation of the posterior cortex of the meta-diaphysis.
 
 Subsequent MRI (Figures 3, 4a, 4b, 4c, and 4d) confirmed a 4.8 cm maximum diameter predominantly fat-signal lesion within the proximal humerus with mildly enhancing internal curvilinear soft tissue stranding and more nodular soft tissue superiorly with speckled intra-lesional fat and extensive full thickness cortical loss and medial cortical permeation with secondary stress fracture. Mildly enhancing mild peri-lesional marrow and cortical oedema were also noted. There was no extra-osseous invasion.
 
 Nuclear medicine whole-body scan revealed avid uptake at the right proximal humerus with no other disease.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 3-month-old girl was brought to casualty with a history of intermittent fever for 1 month, abdominal distension, and straining while passing urine and stools for 4 days. On examination, a soft, tender, non-mobile midline mass was palpated in the suprapubic region.
 Imaging Findings: Transabdominal greyscale ultrasound revealed a large, well-defined cystic lesion in the pelvis with dense mobile echoes (Figure 1). The mass was seen posterior to the bladder and anterior to the rectum and was extending into the abdominal cavity up to the umbilicus. The cystic structure was in continuity with the stretched uterine cavity (Figure 2). Suprapubic probe tenderness was positive. There was no evidence of any solid component or calcifications within. Both the ovaries were seen separately from this lesion. Bilateral kidneys showed mild hydronephrosis. Bowel loops were normal in course, calibre, and peristalsis and were displaced to the periphery. On transperineal ultrasound examination, there was a thin membrane of 1 mm thickness at the vaginal orifice (Figure 3).
PostDescription:

DifferentialDiagnosisList: Pyometrocolpos, Hematometrocolpos, Hydrometrocolpos, Distended urinary bladder, Ovarian neoplasm

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- Age: 3-month-old infant girl.
- Clinical presentation: intermittent fever, abdominal distension, straining on urination and stool.
- Physical exam: well-defined, soft, tender, non-mobile midline suprapubic mass.
- Ultrasound: large well-defined cystic lesion in the pelvis with dense mobile echoes.
- Location: posterior to bladder, anterior to rectum, extending to umbilicus.
- Continuity with stretched uterine cavity.
- No solid components or calcifications.
- Separate ovaries.
- Mild hydronephrosis bilateral.
- Displaced bowel loops.
- Thin membrane of 1 mm at vaginal orifice on transperineal ultrasound.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Pyometrocolpos - Pros: cystic lesion in pelvis, in continuity with uterine cavity, vaginal atresia/obstruction suggested by thin membrane at vaginal orifice, typical for obstructive genital outflow. Cons: pyometrocolpos typically purulent and often presents with signs of infection; thin membrane may not be typical (usually a membrane/septum or atresia).
2) Hematometrocolpos - Pros: cystic lesion continuous with uterine cavity. Cons: usually seen in neonates with vaginal obstruction and maternal estrogen effect causing bleeding; would expect blood in lesion but ultrasound described dense mobile echoes; but no mention of age-related bleed; also thin membrane at vaginal orifice more consistent with obstruction than blood.
3) Hydrometrocolpos - Pros: cystic lesion continuous with uterine and vaginal cavity, typically due to obstructive malformation (imperforate hymen, transverse septum), common in infants, ultrasound findings of well-defined cystic mass behind bladder and anterior to rectum with thin membrane at vaginal orifice fits well. Cons: none significant.
4) Distended urinary bladder - Cons: lesion posterior to bladder, so cannot be bladder; plus continuity with uterine cavity not possible.
5) Ovarian neoplasm - Cons: lesion is midline/cystic, separate from ovaries on US; no solid components or calcifications.

C) Ranking:
- Hydrometrocolpos vs Pyometrocolpos vs Hematometrocolpos:
All three involve cystic pelvic masses with genital tract involvement. The key is the thin membrane at the vaginal orifice seen on transperineal US: this likely represents an obstructive membrane, which is classic for hydrometrocolpos (imperforate hymen or vaginal atresia). The lesion is cystic, no solid components or calcifications, and continuous with the uterus. Mild hydronephrosis can occur due to mass effect. No mention of infection or pus, making pyometrocolpos less likely. No mention or suspicion of bleeding, decreasing likelihood of hematometrocolpos. Thus, the best diagnosis is hydrometrocolpos.
</analysis><final> Hydrometrocolpos </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A middle-aged patient presented with what were initially suspected to be new-onset migraines but then developed auditory and visual hallucinations and complex paranoid beliefs. They became confused and intermittently incoherent with deteriorating coordination, becoming unsteady and sustaining several falls. The clinical picture was further complicated by the onset of myoclonus and a rapidly progressive cognitive decline, which raised suspicion for a neurodegenerative condition.
 Imaging Findings: The patient initially underwent a CT which showed only generalised cerebral atrophy.
 
 They then had further investigations with an MRI, including diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) and corresponding apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) images. This revealed widespread restricted diffusion involving almost the entire cortex except the superior temporal gyrus and also affecting the caudate bilaterally and the left lentiform nuclei. Fluid attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) imaging showed a more subtle high signal in these areas. Other than some small and non-specific foci of high signal in the deep white matter, there were no further positive findings.
PostDescription:

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Middle-aged patient with rapidly progressive cognitive decline, complex psychiatric symptoms (auditory and visual hallucinations, paranoid beliefs), confusion, incoherence, deteriorating coordination, falls, myoclonus.  
- CT showed generalized cerebral atrophy only.  
- MRI DWI shows widespread cortical restricted diffusion almost entire cortex except superior temporal gyrus, bilaterally affecting caudate and left lentiform nuclei.  
- FLAIR shows subtle high signal in the same regions and some non-specific deep white matter changes.  
B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Hypoxic encephalopathy  
   - Pros: Cortical restricted diffusion can be seen on DWI in hypoxic injury.  
   - Cons: Typically involves watershed areas and deep gray matter such as basal ganglia; clinical picture includes acute event of hypoxia; here symptoms develop progressively with psychiatric and neurodegenerative features; widespread cortical involvement except superior temporal gyrus is less typical.  
2) Hyperammonaemia (Hepatic encephalopathy)  
   - Pros: Can cause cortical and basal ganglia edema and restricted diffusion, confusion, and neuropsychiatric symptoms; can cause myoclonus.  
   - Cons: Usually involves more symmetrical and diffuse changes, particularly in insular cortex and thalami; no mention of liver disease or elevated ammonia; pattern less like DWI widespread cortical ribboning.  
3) Hypoglycaemic encephalopathy  
   - Pros: Can cause restricted diffusion in cortex and basal ganglia; can cause confusion and neurological deterioration.  
   - Cons: Usually acute event with known hypoglycemia; no mention of hypoglycemia; the clinical picture is of progressive neurodegeneration rather than acute event; MR pattern not classic.  
4) Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease (CJD)  
   - Pros: Classic MR feature is cortical ribboning on DWI due to cortical restricted diffusion, frequently sparing superior temporal gyrus, involves caudate and putamen; can be asymmetric; patients present with rapidly progressive dementia, myoclonus, psychiatric symptoms, coordination problems.  
   - Cons: CT may be unremarkable early; FLAIR subtle high signal; clinical picture and imaging are highly typical.  
5) Encephalitis  
   - Pros: Can cause cortical diffusion restriction and FLAIR changes; can cause neuropsychiatric symptoms.  
   - Cons: Usually more localized, often temporal lobe involvement (HSV) or limbic system; no mention of fever or lab findings; imaging shows widespread cortical involvement except spared superior temporal gyrus (unusual for herpes encephalitis).  
C) Ranking:  
1) Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease: Pathognomonic imaging finding of cortical ribboning with basal ganglia involvement sparing superior temporal gyrus, clinical picture matches.  
2) Hypoxic encephalopathy: MRI pattern possible but clinical picture less suggestive.  
3) Hepatic encephalopathy: Clinical and imaging less typical, no liver dysfunction mentioned.  
4) Hypoglycaemic encephalopathy: No known hypoglycemia, imaging pattern less typical.  
5) Encephalitis: Clinical and imaging less typical, no fever or inflammatory signs.  
</analysis>  
<final>Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 41-year-old woman presented with worsening perineal pain and pustular skin lesions. Examination revealed extensive erythematous and pustular lesions in the perineal and perianal regions, with rope-like dermal thickening and induration. Multiple fistulous openings with purulent and haemoserous discharge were noted.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound of the perineal and perianal regions revealed dermal and hypodermal band-like structures indicative of fistulas/tunnels, with multiple dermal collections around hair follicles containing hyperechoic hair fragments (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c). Some hypodermal fluid collections were connected to the epidermis via sinus tracts (Figure 1d). Power Doppler showed increased vascularity, suggesting active inflammation (Figure 1e).
 
 MRI demonstrated skin thickening at the perineum and labia majora, hypertrophic scarring, and deep subcutaneous fistulas/tunnels extending into the ischioanal space and perianal region (Figures 2a and 2b), with left adductor muscle involvement suggestive of myositis (Figure 2c). The tunnels exhibited post-contrast rim enhancement, indicating active inflammation, but no fistulas communicating with the anal canal lumen were identified.
 
 After immunomodulatory therapy, significant lesion reduction was observed, with resolution of the fistulas and myositis. Residual skin thickening and scarring were noted, with minimal post-gadolinium enhancement, indicating minor active inflammation (Figures 3a and 3b).

1) Crohn’s disease
   - Pros: Perianal fistulas and sinus tracts are common in Crohn’s; MRI findings of fistulas and inflammation fit.
   - Cons: No fistulas communicating with the anal canal lumen, which is typical but not exclusive in Crohn's; absence of systemic gastrointestinal symptoms or bowel involvement; the presence of hyperechoic hair fragments is not characteristic of Crohn’s.

   - Pros: Classic clinical features of erythema, pustules, dermal thickening, rope-like fibrosis, chronic sinuses and fistulas in apocrine gland-bearing areas (perineal, perianal); hyperechoic hair fragments in dermal collections are characteristic; typical perineal/perianal involvement; improvement with immunomodulatory therapy; MRI shows skin thickening and scarring typical for HS.
   - Cons: None significant.

   - Pros: Presence of fistulas and sinus tracts.
   - Cons: Perianal fistulas are usually secondary to other conditions (e.g., Crohn's); limited to anorectal origin; here, no fistulas communicating with anal canal; clinical picture more extensive with skin lesions and scarring.
4) Fournier gangrene
   - Pros: Necrotizing infection of perineal region.
   - Cons: Fournier gangrene is an acute, severe infection with gas formation and rapid progression; absent necrosis, gas, or systemic toxicity; chronicity and slow improvement with immunomodulation argue against it.
5) Carbuncles
   - Pros: Localized skin infection with pus.
   - Cons: Carbuncles are confined and do not produce multiple fistulas, extensive scarring, or deep sinuses; clinical and imaging findings are systemic and chronic.

1) Hidradenitis suppurativa - best fit clinically and radiologically with characteristic features and treatment response.
2) Crohn’s disease - less likely due to absence of anal canal fistulas and hyperechoic hair fragments, though perianal fistulas are common.
3) Perianal fistula - a feature but not the primary diagnosis.
4) Fournier gangrene - unlikely given chronicity and lack of necrosis.
5) Carbuncles - far less likely given extent and imaging findings.

Final choice: Hidradenitis suppurativa

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 52-year-old male presented to the outpatient clinic with swelling over the right greater trochanteric region, which progressively grew after a fall four months before. He had a previous history of neoadjuvant radiotherapy followed by resection of a soft tissue sarcoma in the left thigh seven years before.
 Imaging Findings: On the preoperative MRI performed seven years before (Figures 1, 2a, 2b, and 2c), an intramuscular lobulated mass was seen in the left tensor fascia lata, extending into the subcutis. The mass showed areas of high signal on T1WI (Figure 2a), which became hypo-intense after fat suppression (Figure 2b). The mass was heterogeneous, predominantly hyperintense on T2WI (Figure 2b) and showed internal areas of heterogeneous enhancement after gadolinium contrast (Figure 2c).
 
 The current MR study revealed a thick-walled, well-defined lesion superficial to the right tensor fascia lata, with heterogeneous high signal on T1WI (Figures 3a and 3b) and T2WI (Figures 4a and 4b), persisting after fat suppression. Multiple internal fluid-fluid levels were present. While post-contrast T1WI sequences (Figure 5) showed intralesional heterogeneous high signal, the dynamic subtraction sequence (Figure 6), only showed a thick peripheral enhancing rim. Therefore, the pseudoenhancement seen on the static sequences was due to a haemorrhage-related high native T1WI signal.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 63-year-old female, with a history of anterior cervical diskectomy and fusion, had a CT head and cervical spine in 2007 for unrelated symptoms. Incidentally, imaging identified a heavily calcified lesion in the right cerebellar hemisphere. The patient remained asymptomatic and, therefore, opted for regular follow-up imaging as opposed to surgical management.
 Imaging Findings: The CT brain demonstrated a 2.5 cm (maximal diameter), highly calcified intra-axial lesion with no surrounding oedema or mass effect, within the right cerebellar fossa (Figures 1 and 2).
 
 The MRI demonstrated the same lesion within the right cerebellar hemisphere, measuring 2.5 cm (maximal axial measurement). The lesion abutted the inferior tentorial surface; however, a broad base against it was not exhibited. There was no notable mass effect or surrounding oedema. On T1 (Figure 3) and T2 (Figure 4) weighted images, the lesion displayed a low signal intensity. Additionally, blooming artefacts were observed on gradient imaging (Figure 5).
 
 During the 17-year follow-up period, the patient exhibited no symptoms. Biennially, MRI heads revealed no change in the lesion size or associated mass effect.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 63-year-old female with a history of anterior cervical diskectomy and fusion.
- Incidentally discovered heavily calcified intra-axial lesion in right cerebellar hemisphere (2.5 cm).
- No surrounding oedema or mass effect; lesion abutting inferior tentorial surface but without broad base attachment.
- Low signal intensity on T1 and T2 weighted MRI.
- Blooming artefacts on gradient imaging suggesting mineralization/fibrous tissue.
- Stable size and appearance over 17 years with biennial MRI follow-up and no symptoms.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Calcified meningioma
   Pros: Common lesion with calcification; can be extra-axial; may abut tentorium.
   Cons: Usually extra-axial with a broad dural base, often causing some mass effect or dural tail; our lesion is intra-axial with no broad tentorial attachment; less likely stable for 17 years without growth; meningiomas usually have iso to hypointense signals on T1 and T2 but less commonly heavily calcified and intra-axial.

2) Calcified cavernoma
   Pros: Typically intra-axial, can show calcification, blooming on gradient due to hemosiderin/fibrous tissue; can be stable long-term.
   Cons: Usually have a mixed signal with hemosiderin rim and sometimes T2 hyperintense core; cavernomas often show a "popcorn" appearance with mixed signal and less likely to be purely low signal with heavy calcification; cavernomas frequently cause small hemorrhages or microbleeds visible on gradient images; also cavernomas typically cause some surrounding gliosis/Oedema over time.

3) Calcified pseudoneoplasm of the neuraxis (CAPNON)
   Pros: Very characteristic for well-circumscribed, heavily calcified intra-axial lesion; low T1 and T2 signal; no mass effect; often stable over long periods; blooming on gradient due to mineralization; no surrounding edema; can be asymptomatic for many years.
   Cons: Rare, but imaging features fit well.

4) Oligodendrogliomas
   Pros: Often calcified intra-axial tumors; can be stable if low grade; T2 hyperintense tumors usually.
   Cons: Usually have some T2 hyperintensity due to tumor cellularity; often show some mass effect or edema; lesion is very calcified and low signal on all sequences, which doesn't fit well with oligodendroglioma typical appearance; also, oligodendrogliomas typically enlarge over years.

5) Choroid plexus papilloma
   Pros: Usually occurs in ventricles (especially 4th ventricle) with possible calcification.
   Cons: This lesion is intra-axial cerebellar parenchyma, not ventricular; no symptoms; choroid plexus papillomas are usually iso/hypointense to CSF on T2, may enhance with gadolinium, not purely calcified; unlikely in cerebellar cortex.

C) Ranking:
1) Calcified pseudoneoplasm of the neuraxis (CAPNON) - fits best: heavily calcified intra-axial lesion, stable over decades, low T1/T2 signals, blooming on gradient, no edema or mass effect, asymptomatic.
2) Calcified cavernoma - intra-axial and calcified but usually has mixed signal and microhemorrhages; lesion lacks typical cavernoma features.
3) Calcified meningioma - less likely given intra-axial location and lack of dural attachment.
4) Oligodendroglioma - less consistent with imaging signs (signal intensity, calcification pattern, growth).
5) Choroid plexus papilloma - improbable given lesion location and imaging characteristics.

<final>Calcified pseudoneoplasm of the neuraxis</final><|return|>
options: Calcified meningioma | Calcified cavernoma | Calcified pseudoneoplasm of the neuraxis | Oligodendrogliomas | Choroid plexus papilloma

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 78-year-old male patient with a history of alcoholism and smoking was referred to the otorhinolaryngology department for dysphagia. Clinical examination revealed a painless 4 cm thickening lesion of the uvula. Cervical palpation revealed no adenopathy. A biopsy of the lesion was performed.
 Imaging Findings: CT scan revealed the presence of a uvula tumour (Figure 1), a suspicious hepatic lesion and a solid cystic lesion in the body of the pancreas. MRI showed a pancreatic body lesion, measuring 22 mm, with cystic and tissue components, intensely enhanced after contrast injection (Figure 2), and a 55 mm heterogeneous hepatic mass, of the segment IV, intensely enhanced after contrast injection (Figure 3). There was no dilatation of the pancreatic or biliary ducts. We performed an FDG PET-CT, which demonstrated an intensely hypermetabolic uvula (SUVmax at 9.2), with moderate hypermetabolism of the adenopathies (SUVmax at 2.5) and the liver lesion (SUVmax at 5.4) (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 47-year-old female patient presented at the Emergency Department with severe acute abdominal pain associated with nausea and vomiting. She also referred cessation of flatus and bowel movements for 4 days. Past medical history is unremarkable. On physical exam, the abdomen was distended and tender with no rebound or guarding.
 Imaging Findings: A plain erect abdominal radiograph revealed the presence of an air-distended large bowel loop in the left upper quadrant consistent with the inverted cecum, and multiple fluid levels (Figure 1).
 
 An abdominal contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CT) was later performed, with coronal and sagittal reconstructions (Figures 2, 3a, 3b, 4a, 4b, and 5). OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Our patient is a 64-year-old male admitted to the intensive care unit (ICU) due to a prolonged cardiac arrest, who was on mechanical circulatory support with extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO). He was COVID-19-negative and presented with an encephalopathic syndrome.
 Imaging Findings: A brain magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was performed, and the susceptibility-weighted imaging sequence (SWI), revealed multiple small hypointense foci consistent with cerebral microbleeds (CBMs). These lesions were diffusely distributed across the subcortical white matter, splenium of the corpus callosum and posterior fossa, affecting the brainstem (midbrain and pons), as well as both cerebellar hemispheres. The cortex and periventricular regions were spared (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c). In addition, we found small watershed infarctions (Figures 2a and 2b).
PostDescription:

The cortex and periventricular regions were spared. In addition, we found small watershed infarctions.

A) Key findings:
- 64-year-old male with prolonged cardiac arrest and ECMO support.
- Encephalopathic syndrome.
- Brain MRI SWI sequence shows multiple small hypointense foci consistent with cerebral microbleeds (CBMs).
- Distribution of microbleeds: subcortical white matter, splenium of corpus callosum, posterior fossa (brainstem: midbrain and pons, cerebellar hemispheres).
- Sparing of cortex and periventricular regions.
- Small watershed infarctions present.

1) Critical illness-associated cerebral microbleeds:
   Pros: Patient with prolonged ICU stay, ECMO support, encephalopathy; distribution matches typical pattern (subcortical white matter, brainstem, cerebellum), sparing cortex/periventricular; small watershed infarcts compatible with hypoperfusion state; fits clinical scenario of critical illness.
   Cons: None significant.

2) Hypertensive encephalopathy:
   Pros: Could lead to microbleeds, often in deep gray matter (basal ganglia, thalami).
   Cons: Distribution in this case is mainly subcortical WM, brainstem, cerebellum, sparing periventricular and cortex; no deep gray matter involvement typical for hypertensive microbleeds; no mention of hypertension crisis; less likely.

   Pros: Typically microbleeds in lobar cortico-subcortical regions; common in older adults.
   Cons: Cortex spared here; lesions mainly subcortical white matter and posterior fossa; CAA rarely involves brainstem and cerebellum; clinical context unlikely.

4) Fat-emboli:
   Pros: Can cause microhemorrhages; possible in trauma or fat embolism syndrome.
   Cons: No trauma history; distribution and pattern not typical; less likely in this ICU patient with cardiac arrest and ECMO.

5) COVID-19:
   Pros: Known to cause microvascular brain injury and microbleeds.
   Cons: Patient COVID-19 negative; less likely.

C) Ranking:
1) Critical illness-associated cerebral microbleeds (matches clinical scenario, distribution, and MRI findings perfectly).
2) Fat-emboli (possible but less likely given clinical context and distribution).
3) Hypertensive encephalopathy (distribution atypical).
4) Cerebral amyloid angiopathy (distribution and clinical context not supporting).
5) COVID-19 (patient negative status excludes).

Final ranking: 1) Critical illness-associated cerebral microbleeds.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old woman presented with progressive shortness of breath and chest pain for 3 days. She was diagnosed with thrombosis involving the left popliteal vein to the common femoral vein with the possibility of a slow-flow vascular malformation of the left popliteal region. She was referred to our institute for further management.
 Imaging Findings: USG (ultrasonogram)
 
 : Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) involving the left popliteal vein (Figure 3a), superficial and common femoral veins (Figure 3b) with heteroechoic lesion around the popliteal vessels (Figure 3c).
 
 CTLL angiogram (computed tomography lower limb)
 
 : A lobulated heterogeneously enhancing lesion (Figure 4a) in the distal left thigh and proximal leg (intramuscular and intermuscular plane), extending into the popliteal vein (Figure 4b), superficial femoral, common femoral and common iliac veins (Figure 4c).
 
 CTPA (computed tomography pulmonary angiogram)
 
 : Eccentric filling defect involving the main and right pulmonary arteries (Figure 1a), extending to the right interlobar artery (Figure 1b), right basal trunk (Figure 1c), and segmental branches of the right upper lobe and left basal trunk [1]. Thrombus noted in the right atrium and ventricle. Multiple cavitary consolidations involving bilateral lower lobe segments (Figure 2).
 
 PET-CT (positron emission tomography – computed tomography)
 
 : High-grade metabolic activity in the left popliteal and thigh lesion (Figure 5b) and in the deep venous tumour thrombus (Figure 5a), bilateral pulmonary arteries (Figure 6b), right atrium (Figure 6a) and ventricle.

A) Key findings:
- Young woman (20 years old) with progressive shortness of breath and chest pain.
- Progressive thrombosis from left popliteal vein to common femoral and common iliac veins.
- Imaging shows a lobulated heterogeneously enhancing intramuscular and intermuscular lesion extending into deep veins.
- Evidence of tumor thrombus extending to right atrium and ventricle.
- CTPA shows pulmonary artery emboli with a thrombus in the right heart chambers.
- PET-CT shows high-grade metabolic activity in the left thigh lesion, deep venous thrombus, bilateral pulmonary arteries, and right heart chambers.
- Initial suspicion was slow-flow vascular malformation but imaging shows a heterogeneously enhancing lesion with metabolic activity and tumor thrombus.
- Presence of intravascular lesion with extension into major veins and heart chambers, plus pulmonary artery emboli with metabolic activity, suggests malignant tumor invading vasculature.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1) Primary intravascular synovial sarcoma:
Pros: Synovial sarcoma can present as a heterogeneously enhancing mass and may involve vessels; can show high PET uptake.
Cons: Synovial sarcoma is usually located near joints but not typically intravascular or with extensive vascular infiltration and tumor thrombus extending to heart chambers.

2) Slow flow vascular malformation:
Pros: Initially considered due to limb swelling and thrombosis.
Cons: Vascular malformations are typically non-enhancing or slowly enhancing, no high metabolic activity on PET-CT, no tumor thrombus, no solid mass invading veins or heart chambers.

3) Leiomyosarcoma:
Pros: Can arise from vascular smooth muscle, can be intravascular, commonly involves IVC and can extend into right heart and cause pulmonary emboli; lesions enhance heterogeneously; high PET uptake.
Cons: Leiomyosarcoma typically affects older patients, but can occur in young adults.

4) Vascular leiomyomatosis:
Pros: A benign intravascular smooth muscle proliferation, can extend into heart chambers; can cause thrombosis.
Cons: Typically bland on imaging, no high-grade metabolic activity on PET-CT; not malignant or high PET uptake.

5) Tumor thrombus:
Pros: Imaging shows lesion invading veins and right heart chambers, PET-CT shows high metabolic activity in thrombus - consistent with malignant tumor thrombus.
Cons: Tumor thrombus is a descriptive term rather than a primary diagnosis; needs to be combined with tumor type - likely syncs with another diagnosis.

6) Lymphoma:
Pros: Can cause thrombosis and can form intravascular masses.
Cons: Typically does not form lobulated intramuscular masses with extension into veins and heart chambers; PET uptake can be high but vascular extension is unusual.

C) Ranking based on best fit:
- Primary intravascular synovial sarcoma: possible but less likely due to typical location and rarity intravascular.
- Slow flow vascular malformation: excluded based on PET and aggressive behavior.
- Leiomyosarcoma: fits well with intravascular mass, thrombosis, heart and pulmonary artery involvement, and high PET uptake.
- Vascular leiomyomatosis: less likely due to lack of high metabolic activity.
- Tumor thrombus: descriptive of findings but not a primary diagnosis.
- Lymphoma: less likely given intravascular aggressive features.

Final choice: Leiomyosarcoma because it best matches the imaging features of a heterogeneously enhancing intravascular lesion with high metabolic activity invading major veins, right heart chambers, and causing pulmonary emboli. This aggressive behavior and intravascular tumor thrombus is most characteristic of leiomyosarcoma.

</analysis><final>Leiomyosarcoma</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 26-year-old male with severe haemophilia A presented to the emergency department with a month-long history of haematuria, dysuria, and lower abdominal pain. The patient is an active smoker and reported a weight loss of 20 kg over the last year. The patient admitted recreational use of ketamine.
 Imaging Findings: Initial ultrasound imaging revealed a low bladder volume with symmetrical bladder wall thickening (Figure 1a) and bilateral hydronephrosis.
 
 CT intravenous urography (IVU) demonstrated mucosal enhancement and confirmed symmetrical bladder wall thickening, forming the classic “hourglass bladder deformity” (Figures 1b and 1c). Additionally, ureteral wall thickening, proximal urinary tract dilation, and hydronephrosis were observed (Figure 2).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 50-year-old patient is referred from the primary care due to epigastric pain radiating to the left flank, accompanied by asthenia and episodes of postprandial nausea, as well as signs of panniculitis in both lower limbs. Blood tests revealed elevated CA 19-9 levels (135.5 U/mL).
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced CT scan was performed, revealing a hypodense lesion with cystic appearance in contact with the anteroinferior margin of the pancreatic tail, with areas of higher density inside it, which are suggestive of septations (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 A contrast-enhanced MRI was conducted to further characterise the lesion. It showed a heterogeneous tumour with an atypical cystic appearance, related to higher signal intensity on T1-weighted and lower intensity on T2-weighted images, when compared to free water and the presence of small cystic rounded areas with higher intensity on T2-weighted images (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 Some small foci of signal drop on out-of-phase T1-weighted sequences compared with in-phase T1-weighted sequences were observed inside the lesion (Figures 3a and 3b). Additionally, areas of diffusion restriction were noted within the lesion (Figures 3c and 3d) and contrast-enhancing septations (Figure 3e). There was no visible communication with the main pancreatic duct.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 27-year-old male with progressive imbalance while walking and generalised tonic-clonic seizures from 5 years of age. No significant family history. Neurological examination revealed lower limb spasticity, brisk reflexes, extensor plantar reflex, features of ataxia, impaired pain, and touch sensations. Ophthalmic and fundus examinations were normal.
 Imaging Findings: MRI brain revealed the atrophy of the superior cerebellar vermis and the atrophy of the superior cerebellar cortex. The inferior vermis and the inferior cerebellar cortex are spared (Figures 2 and 3). T2/FLAIR linear hypointense stripes in the pons at the level of superior and middle cerebellar peduncle giving a tigroid/striped appearance (Figures 1a and 1b). Atrophy of the cervical and thoracic spinal cord is seen (Figure 3). Mildly atrophic pre-central and post-central gyri (Figure 4) with the thinned-out body of corpus callosum (Figure 2) also seen.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 44-year-old woman with a history of controlled asthma and splenectomy following a gunshot wound 30 years prior presented with a dry cough, occasionally producing yellow-green sputum. She reported intermittent moderate dyspnoea and wheezing on auscultation, with symptoms gradually worsening over the past 30 days.
 Imaging Findings: Chest computed tomography (CCT) revealed solid nodules with homogeneous attenuation and enhancement (Figure 1), smooth borders, no calcifications (Figure 2), and no distortion of the adjacent lung parenchyma within the left pleural space (Figure 3). The nodules were randomly distributed along the longitudinal axis and measured up to 5.9 cm at the base of the hemithorax (Figure 4). A previous splenectomy was confirmed by the absence of the spleen in the abdominal cavity (Figure 5).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 76-year-old male patient with a prior diagnosis of cerebral amyloid angiopathy (CAA) was referred to the emergency department from a nursing home due to progressive clinical deterioration over the past 24 hours. The patient presented decreased consciousness. No fever or recent trauma was reported. An urgent CT scan was requested (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 4).
 Imaging Findings: Non-contrast CT performed four years earlier at the onset of CAA (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1d) showed an isolated convexity subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH) in the right frontal sulcus without trauma, in a patient with mild cognitive impairment.
 
 In the MRI one month after SAH, susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) sequence (Figure 2), significant superficial haemosiderosis and multiple supra and infratentorial microhaemorrhages are observed as hypointense areas with a “blooming” effect.
 
 Urgent CT (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 4) demonstrated an intraparenchymal haematoma in the right basal ganglia, a smaller left frontal haemorrhage, and extensive hypodensity in the left supratentorial white matter suggestive of cerebral oedema.
 
 In the MRI, two weeks after the acute event and corticosteroid treatment, hyperintensities in white matter on T2-weighted (Figures 5a, 5b, and 5c) and FLAIR (Figures 5d and 5e) sequences are observed, without diffusion restriction (Figures 5f and 5g), corresponding to vasogenic oedema with improvement compared to the previous CT (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, and 4).
 
 CT one month after the acute event (Figures 6a, 6b, 6c, 6d and 7) showed near-complete resolution of cerebral oedema, confirming the response to corticosteroid treatment.
